ØAlbus Dumbledore abused his authority, violated the sanctity of Hogwarts, manipulated the Wizengamot, engineered war, and presided over the systematic erosion of wizarding culture.Æ Silence fell. Not stunned. Not disbelieving. Grieving. Marvoloís lips curved, just barely - It was all going to plan /// The Great Hall hummed before Harrison even crossed the threshold. It wasnít loudînot exactlyîbut it was alive in that way that only came when something momentous had happened and everyone knew it at once. Whispers skimmed across the long tables like wind over water, stalling and surging as students leaned together, eyes flicking toward the entrance with barely disguised curiosity. Luna drifted in beside him, serene as ever, fingers laced loosely through his sleeve as though the attention meant nothing at all. Newspapers. Stacks of them lay scattered along the tables, some already opened, others abandoned mid-breakfast as students read and reread the same lines, expressions ranging from horrified to vindicated to quietly awed. A group of second-years stared at one headline like it might bite them. Harrison reached the Ravenclaw table and plucked up a copy of the Daily Prophet with practiced ease. The headline took up nearly the entire front page. ALBUS DUMBLEDORE TRIED IN ABSENTIA WIZENGAMOT HEARS SHOCKING EVIDENCE IS BRITAINíS GREATEST LIGHT ITS TRUE DARK LORD? Harrison blinked once. Rita didnít even spell my name wrong this time.Æ Luna peered over his shoulder, her smile slow and luminous. ØSheís very excited when the world rearranges itself,Æ she observed. ØI think she feeds on it.Æ ØI should send her a fruit basket,Æ Harrison mused. ØAs thanks.Æ Luna nodded seriously. ØGrapes would be nice. For clarity. Maybe add some chocolate pudding as well.Æ Around them, the whispers grew bolder. -they tried him without him there.-did you see the part about compulsions?.-my gran always said.-true dark lord, can you imagine-Harrison slid onto the bench, Luna settling beside him as though this were any other morning. A hesitant voice broke through the noise. ØHarrison?Æ He looked up to see Cho Chang standing a few feet away, plate clutched in both hands, dark eyes worried. A few of her friends hovered just behind her, clearly listening in. ØAre youè are you worried?Æ she asked carefully. ØAbout Professor- about Dumbledore coming after you again? After what happened at the ball?Æ The Hall seemed to lean in. Harrison considered her for a moment, then smiledîeasy, genuine, and utterly calm. ØNo,Æ he said simply. Cho blinked. ØNo?Æ ØNo,Æ he repeated. ØI trust the teachers. Professor McGonagall has Hogwarts locked down tighter than Gringotts, and the wards are the strongest theyíve been in centuries.Æ He shrugged lightly. ØHogwarts is safe.Æ That much was true. What he didnít say - what he didnít need to say - was that if Albus Dumbledore did try to come for him, Harrison would welcome it. He was tired of running. Tired of waiting. Ready for the long shadow to finally end. Luna squeezed his arm, as though she felt the edge of that thought, and smiled up at Cho. ØBesides,Æ she added cheerfully, Øpeople who rely on fear never notice when the world has moved on without them.Æ Cho hesitated, then nodded, visibly confused but seemingly reassured. ØIím glad,Æ she said softly, before moving away. Harrison unfolded the paper again, scanning the column where Dumbledoreís carefully curated legend unraveled line by line. He took a sip of pumpkin juice, unbothered by the stares, the whispers, the dawning realization across the Hall that something fundamental had shifted. ØBreakfast reading is getting rather dramatic,Æ he said lightly. Luna smiled. ØHistory usually is.Æ A commotion from the gryffindor table broke the whispers. Ron Weasley, more of a ghost than a boy since the first task, stormed out of the hall. ØIt would be a shame,Æ Luna muttered, drawing Harrison's attention. ØOh?Æ ØMmmm, I find myself unable to feel sorry for him, but at the same time, I wonderèÆ her eyes seemed to wander, Øthe nargles always were fond of him.Æ /// Ron stared at the paper until the words blurred. Heíd read the headline three times already, each pass slower than the last, as though the words might be lying to him and eventually confess. ALBUS DUMBLEDORE TRIED IN ABSENTIA EVIDENCE OF COMPULSIONS, WAR-MONGERING, CULTURAL ERASURE His hands were shaking. Around him, the Great Hall buzzed - whispers overlapping, benches scraping, students craning their necks toward Harrison Potter-Black like he was some kind of living prophecy .but Ron barely heard any of it. All he could see were the moving photographs, the testimony summaries, the cold, official language that stripped the man heíd trusted down to something ugly and unrecognisable. Compulsions. On students. On adults. On supporters. Ronís stomach twisted violently. ØNo,Æ he muttered under his breath. ØNo, no, noèÆ Memories surfaced unbidden -anger that never quite felt like his own, the sharp certainty that Harry had been wrong, dangerous, dark. The way his thoughts had always slid so neatly into place whenever Dumbledore spoke. The ease with which doubt had turned into conviction. He dropped the paper as though it burned. The sound echoed louder than it should have. Someone at the Gryffindor table glanced at him. Then another. Murmurs shifted direction, following the way Ron pushed back from the bench so hard it screeched against the stone floor. ØRon-?Æ someone started. He didnít stop. He stood, chair toppling behind him, and walked -no, strode- toward the doors. Heads turned. Whispers followed. -thatís Weasley.-he was on Dumbledoreís side.-did you see his face-Ron kept his eyes forward. For once, he didnít care what they thought. The doors swung open and slammed shut behind him with a finality that made several students flinch. /// The hospital wing smelled the same as always -clean, sharp, faintly floral- but it no longer felt safe. It felt like a place where truths were dragged into the light whether you were ready for them or not. Madam Pomfrey looked up sharply as Ron marched in. ØMr Weasley, you should be resting-Æ ØI want to be tested,Æ Ron said, voice hoarse but unyielding. She frowned. ØTested for what?Æ ØFor everything,Æ he snapped, then swallowed, forcing himself to steady. ØPlease. My core. My mind. I need to know.Æ Pomfrey studied him for a long moment, then sighed and gestured toward a bed. ØSit.Æ The spells were quiet. Clinical. Blue and silver magic tracing paths Ron could feel but not see, sinking into him, mapping what remained of his power. Pomfreyís expression changed. Subtly at first - then unmistakably. ØOh, RonaldèÆ she murmured. Heartbreaking at the evidence before her. His chest tightened. ØWhat?Æ She hesitated, then waved her wand again, slower this time, more carefully. The magic lingered around his core, probing gently. ØYour magical core is severely damaged,Æ she said at last. ØThe dragonfire destabilised it, butèÆ She paused. ØThere are scars here. Old ones. New ones.Æ Ronís breath caught. ØScars from what?Æ Pomfrey looked him straight in the eye. ØCompulsion magic,Æ she said quietly. ØRepeated exposure. Long-term.Æ The words landed like a curse. ØBut-Æ Ron shook his head. ØI donít feel - thereís nothing there now.Æ ØNo,Æ Pomfrey agreed. ØWhatever was attached to you is gone. When your core collapsed, it tore the compulsions away with it.Æ Ron laughed weakly. ØSo whatè it took my magic and returned my free will on the way out?Æ Pomfrey didnít scold him for the tone. ØIn a manner of speaking.Æ Silence stretched. ØSo I wasnít crazy,Æ Ron said finally. ØI wasnít justè obsessed.Æ ØNo,Æ she said firmly. ØYou were manipulated.Æ He closed his eyes. All that loyalty. All that anger. All the times heíd looked at Harry and thought he was the problem. It hadnít even been him. ØWhat happens now?Æ Ron asked. ØI will give this evidence to the DMLE, with your permission. You may be asked to give evidence.Æ Pomfreyís voice softened. ØIf you would like, I will call for your father, have him come and support you.Æ ØNo. I mean, don't call him. Just give the evidence.Æ Poppy hesitated, ØMr Weasley, what was done to you is horrific. I can't begin to imagine how you feel.Ø- I feel nothing.Æ The words hit harder than the boy realised, ØI will deliver the files, and call for your family.Æ she said, but as she turned away Ron asked another question. ØWill I get better? My magic - now they're gone.Æ Poppy wished she could lie, that she could change the truth but she knew doing so would be a disservice, ØNo. Magical cores are wondrous things, but even they can't recover from everything. What was done to you, it is permanent.Æ Ron nodded, Øif i continue the tournamentèÆ ØIf you continue in the Tournament, the strain could finish destroying whatís left of your core. But If you withdrawè" Poppy didn't finish, she didn't know what potion was worse. ØIíll lose it,Æ Ron said. ØAll of it.Æ ØYes.Æ He nodded slowly. No hesitation. ØThen Iím done,Æ he said. ØIím not competing again.Æ Pomfrey stiffened. ØRon-Æ ØIím not dying for magic that lied to me,Æ he said, voice low but steady. ØIf that means becoming a squib, then fine. Maybe being normal isnít the worst thing in the world.Æ He swung his legs off the bed and stood, a little unsteady but determined. ØI choose me,Æ Ron said quietly. ØFor once.Æ Pomfrey watched him go, heart heavy. Ron Weasley walked out of the infirmary with his back straight, his path chosen - not because it was easy, but because it was finally his own. /// Amelia Bones had thought she was prepared. She had seen pensieve memories that made seasoned Aurors retch. She had read testimony that shattered families and ended legacies. She had watched the myth of Albus Dumbledore unravel thread by careful thread. Still - this made her hands go cold. The parchment lay on her desk, stamped with the seal of Hogwartsí hospital wing and counter-signed by two separate Healers. Clinical. Precise. Impossible to argue with. SUBJECT: Ronald Bilius Weasley FINDINGS: Magical core destabilisation consistent with high-intensity elemental exposure. ADDITIONAL OBSERVATION: Residual scarring indicative of long-term, layered compulsion magic. Evidence of magical leeching. STATUS: Compulsions no longer active. Likely torn free during core collapse. Amelia stared at the words. ØA child,Æ she whispered. Not a political rival. Not a dangerous radical. A child - one of many Albus had no doubt twisted. Her jaw tightened as understanding clicked into place with sickening clarity. The compulsions werenít just about loyalty. They were placement. Direction. Pressure. Nudges at the right moment to ensure obedience, rivalry, division. The boy hadnít merely been manipulated into belief - he had been used as a lever against another child. Against Harrison. It didn't surprise her that it came back to him. She didn't blame him. ØAnd then stood by while the world shattered the pieces.Æ Amelia leaned back slowly, staring up at the ceiling as though it might offer absolution. It did not. Albus Dumbledore had not merely abused power. He had engineered suffering - and when it became inconvenient, he had let it rot. Her expression hardened into something like iron. ØSo be it,Æ she murmured. ØYou will answer for every single person youíve harmed.Æ /// Arthur Weasley arrived at Hogwarts with a smile already prepared. Forced, perhaps - but hopeful. Ron had survived dragonfire. That alone felt like a miracle. Whatever came next, they would face it together. They always did. Minerva McGonagall met him just outside the hospital wing. She had taught alongside Albus Dumbledore for decades. Defended him. Trusted him with her life, with children. And here sat a father, breaking apart because that trust had been weaponized. Minervaís hands clenched in her sleeves. Disgust was too small a word. This was betrayal on a scale she had no language for. ØI will not forgive him,Æ Minerva said quietly, the words edged with steel. Whatever path he walks now - it will be his. Free of anyoneís chains.Æ Arthur nodded weakly. Minerva turned away then, because if she didnít, she might scream. Chapter End Notes Iím honestly blown away every single time by the sheer amount of comments and support this story gets. As always, Iím incredibly grateful, and genuinely a little awed, by how many people have read, followed, and cared about this fic. Youíre all amazing, and I love you for it. That said, there are a few things I want to address, because some of the comments here and on my other works have beenè wild. Donít get me wrong, about 90% of what I receive is pure love, but some of the criticism has been intense enough that youíd think I was being paid to write this. Which brings me to the main point. I started writing this story as a teenager nearly ten years ago. At the time, I did have a plan, and for the most part Iíve followed it. However, as the years have gone on, that plan has shifted, because honestly, it wasnít a great one. Or rather, it was an immature one. Some of the tropes, relationships, and character motivations I once intended no longer resonate with me, and that has made writing this fic increasingly difficult. Resulting in, as many of you will know, long periods of inactivity. I want to give this story an ending that feels authentic and earned. And while the ending I originally planned no longer fits the story or the writer I am now, I want to give you all something that doesn't feel rushed. Chapter50 Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes ~ Previously /// Minervaís hands clenched in her sleeves. Disgust was too small a word. This was betrayal on a scale she had no language for. ØI will not forgive him,Æ Minerva said quietly, the words edged with steel. ØNot for this. Not ever.Æ Arthur looked up at her, eyes red, searching. Minerva met his gaze without flinching. ØYou are not alone,Æ she said. ØAnd neither is Ron. Whatever path he walks now - it will be his. Free of anyoneís chains.Æ Arthur nodded weakly. Minerva turned away then, because if she didnít, she might scream. /// Minerva McGonagall sat behind her desk and pondered - She long expected to inherit the title of headmistress from Albus, but she had never imagined doing it like this... The office still held the faint traces of his presence - from the lingering scent of lemon, to the assortment of knickknacks she hadn't quite had the time to sort. ØHi, Dad.Æ They sat at Minervaís gesture. Aboutè giving up your magic.Æ Ron nodded once, scarred face resolute ØBut I want to hear it from you,Æ Arthur continued. ØIs this really what you want?Æ ØYes,Æ Ron said immediately. No hesitation. ØRon - if youíre doing this because you feel cornered, or because you think this is punishment-Æ ØItís not a punishment,Æ Ron said flatly. ØIt's a choice.Æ George studied him closely. ØGood luck with that.Æ It wasnít sarcastic. It also wasnít heartfelt. George added, ØYeah. Hope it works out." Ron glanced at them, perhaps searching for something more - reconciliation, maybe. He didnít find it. And for the first time, Fred realised he didnít care. As Ron turned back to Arthur, George leaned slightly toward his twin. ØWell,Æ he murmured under his breath, Øthatís that.Æ Fred followed Ronís retreating gaze, then shrugged internally. ØI feel a bit bad for him,Æ he admitted quietly. ØBeing messed with like that.Æ ØMm,Æ George agreed. ØStill.Æ Still, Ron had made his choices. Still, the damage lingered. And still - Fred felt a surprising, undeniable sense of relief. Relief that Ron would no longer cause issues. ØHe wonít be our problem anymore,Æ George finished. Fred nodded. Whatever sympathy they had was outweighed by certainty. This was an ending Ron had earned. And they were more than ready to move on without him. /// The Hogwarts library was unusually quiet for the late afternoon. Sunlight glittered through the tall windows in pale gold ribbons, dust motes drifting lazily between towering shelves, students whispering in quiet contemplation. Harrison sat at one of the long tables near the back - parchment hazardously thrown around him, beside him, a book on old ritual theory open but largely ignored. Books once banned by Dumbledore had started to reappear in the library, and he was happy to see he wasn't the only one taking advantage of them. Neville sat close at his side, shoulder nearly brushing Harrisonís arm, quietly annotating a herbology text which would have once landed him in detention for a week. Hannah and Susan occupied the opposite side of the table, heads bent together as they whispered over a DMLE compendium, one which Dumbledore would have rather seen burn than studied .Susan correcting, Hannah listening intently. The house felt wrong without the noise - too quiet, too still, as though it were holding its breath - He didnít bother taking off his coat. Instead, he went straight to the small writing desk near the kitchen window, pulled out parchment, and sat. His hands shook as he wrote. My Boys, Iím sorry. Iím sorry I didnít see what was happening. Dad He read it once. Folded it. Sent it by floo to Bills address before he could second-guess himself. Arthur walked past familiar healers who now wouldnít quite meet his eyes. Mollyís room was at the end of the corridor, warded, monitored, humming softly with spellwork meant to hold together what little stability remained. She was awake. Too awake. I sever all marital, magical, and household bonds.Æ The spell hit like thunder. The machines around Molly screamed. Lights flared crimson, gauges spinning wildly as the magic sustaining her core destabilised .then collapsed in on itself. Molly cried out, arching as the last remnants of her power tore free, leaving behind only emptiness. Healers shouted outside as alarms blared and footsteps thundered toward the room. Her core - itís gone!Æ Arthur turned to them, face pale but resolute. ØSheís a squib now,Æ the healer whispered, horrified, the words tumbling out in response to their shock. ØShe can be like Ron,Æ he continued. ØMaybe then sheíll understand what she helped take from him.Æ He turned and walked out as alarms continued to shriek behind him, each step lighter than the last. He had been broken when he found out what Molly had done, now though, now he felt free. /// Calypso stood watching as Arthur Weasley walked away, torn between disbelief and pride for him. What he had just done would see him judged in the court of public opinion, but she had heard what Molly No-Name had admitted. For a moment she wished her vows allowed her to tell people what she knew. Watching him walk away, seeing the strength return to his steps in response to his conviction, she felt a sense of relief- relief knowing that he hadn't been broken by what had happened. /// Bill apparated to the Burrow so quickly that he nearly splinched himself. The letter had been too short. Too apologetic. Too final. Iím sorry. I will respect your distance. That wasnít a normal Weasley letter. That was the sort of thing people wrote when they were about to do something irreversible. Bill didnít even bother knocking. He burst through the door, wand already in his hand. ØDad?Æ The Burrow was silent. For one horrifying moment, Bill thought he was too late. Then he heard it. Crying. Not the loud, explosive kind he remembered from childhood arguments - this was quieter. Broken. The sound of a man who had held himself together for too long and finally run out of strength. Bill followed it to the kitchen. His dad sat at the table, elbows braced against the wood, head in his hands. His shoulders shook. His wand lay discarded, just out of reach beside a cold cup of tea. Relief hit Bill so hard his knees nearly gave out. ØDad,Æ he said hoarsely. Arthur looked up, eyes red-rimmed and unfocused. ØBill.Æ Bill crossed the room in three strides and pulled his father into a fierce hug. Arthur stiffened for half a second - then collapsed into it, gripping Billís robes like a lifeline. ØYou scared me,Æ Bill muttered, pressing his forehead to Arthurís hair. ØI thought-Æ ØI know,Æ Arthur rasped. ØIím sorry.Æ They stayed like that for a long moment, until Arthurís breathing evened out and the shaking stopped. Bill pulled back just enough to look at him. ØAll right. Start talking. Slowly.Æ Arthur nodded, swallowing hard. ØRonís withdrawn from the Tournament,Æ he said. ØHeísè heís letting his magic go. His core was damaged. And there were compulsions on him, Bill. Long-term ones.Æ Billís stomach dropped. ØCompulsions,Æ he repeated. ØOn Ron.Æ Arthur nodded. ØFrom Dumbledore.Æ Bill closed his eyes briefly, rage burning behind them. Heíd suspected manipulation - heíd felt something was wrong - but hearing it confirmed made his hands curl into fists. ØAnd Mum?Æ Bill asked quietly. Arthur hesitated. Then he said, ØShe helped him.Æ The words felt unreal. ØShe knew,Æ Arthur continued. ØShe believed him. Thought she was protecting Ron. I confronted her.Æ Bill opened his eyes. ØAnd?Æ ØI disavowed her,Æ Arthur said. His voice didnít waver this time. ØThe bond breaking destabilised what was left of her core. Sheís a squib now.Æ Silence crashed down between them. Bill stared at his father, shock slowly giving way to something colder - Sharper. ØShe helped control her own son,Æ Bill said flatly. ØYes.Æ ØAnd now she knows what that cost him.Æ Arthur nodded once. Bill exhaled slowly. Relief flickered again - stronger this time. Not relief for Molly. Never that. Relief that Arthur was still standing. Still himself. That he hadnít accepted this injustice and called it love. His father was not a weak man, he knew that, but he had let a lot go in the name of love. ØIím glad youíre okay,Æ Bill said sincerely. Arthur looked at him, eyes glassy. ØI donít feel okay.Æ ØI know,Æ Bill replied. ØBut you will be.Æ Bill sank into the chair opposite him, rubbing a hand over his face. ØRon,Æ he murmured. ØMerlin. What a mess.Æ ØHeís resolute,Æ Arthur said. ØDetached -But determined- He doesnít want to be a pawn again and he doesn't want to risk dying in a competition he didn't enter.Æ Bill nodded slowly. ØThat sounds like Ron, actually. When he finally decides something.Æ He paused, then added quietly, ØIím worried about his future.Æ ØSo am I,Æ Arthur admitted. Bill leaned back, staring at the ceiling beams, as he processed. Then, against his will, something ugly and honest twisted in his chest. Vindication. Not joy. Not pride. But a grim, vindictive satisfaction. ØShe always thought she knew best,Æ Bill said softly. ØAlways thought love meant control.Æ Arthur said nothing. Bill looked back at his father. ØI wonít pretend Iím sad for her.Æ Arthur met his gaze. ØNeither will I,Æ he replied. The words sat heavy - but right. Bill stood and squeezed Arthurís shoulder. ØYou did the right thing.Æ Arthur let out a long, shaky breath. ØStay,Æ he said quietly. Bill nodded without hesitation. ØOf course.Æ As the Burrow creaked around them, Bill resolved two things with perfect clarity: Ron would need someone steady in his corner now. And whatever Molly Weasley had been-Whatever she had became-She no longer had a place in their lives. /// Molly Weasley screamed. The sound tore out of her raw and broken, echoing off the walls of the St Mungoís ward until healers rushed past her door and then - wisely - kept their distance. The wards flared softly, holding, containing. Caging her. ØNo - NO - this isnít happening!Æ she shrieked, fingers clawing at the sheets as though she could dig her magic back out of them by force. ØI felt it - I felt it leave!Æ There was nothing. No warmth beneath her skin. No familiar hum in her bones. No comforting pulse of power that had been with her since childhood, since her first accidental charm had sent a teapot spinning through the air. Nothing. A hollow. A void. A silence so vast it made her head ring. ØI am NOT a squib!Æ she screamed again, voice cracking, hysteria edging onto madness. ØI am Molly Prewett - Molly Weasley - I have MAGIC!Æ She flung her hand out, instinctively pulling, reaching for her wand. Nothing happened. The wand lay dead, not even twitching in her fingers. Arthurís face - cold enough to burn, his resolve. The words of disavowal snapping through the air like a curse. The way her core had collapsed -not burned, not attacked - simplyè fallen apart. Gone. A sob tore free, quickly morphing into manic laughter. ØOh, this is his doing,Æ she gasped, eyes wild. ØThis is Albusís fault. ØAfter everything I did for him - after everything - I SAVED him!Æ The truth slammed into her then, brutal and unavoidable. Ron had chosen this. He had walked away from magic. From her. Now she lay alone in a ward, magicless, abandoned, mind fraying under the weight of her choices. And somewhere deep inside the wreckage of her thoughts, a single, unbearable truth echoed again and again: She had sacrificed her son, her daughter .And in the end, the Light had not saved her. It had devoured her. /// The staff room was tense - even the enchanted windows showed a grey, brooding sky that matched the mood within. ØThank you for coming on such short notice,Æ she said crisply. ØThis will not take long, but it is important.Æ That alone set several of them on edge. Very interesting indeed. /// Amelia Bones stood very still as Alastor Moody finished speaking. And Amelia Bones vowed, as she returned to her desk and reached for the next damning file marked Albus Percival Wulfric Brian Dumbledore, that Ron Weasley would be the last. /// Marvolo listened without interrupting. No, he would commit atrocities with a smile if Harrison asked, and feel not even an iota of guilt. His darling soulmate had been targeted, undermined, and even pushed toward death by the same manipulations that had ensnared Weasley. Many would call vengeance appropriate. But Harrison was not many. He was kind where Marvolo was not, rational compared to Marvolo's own madness. Ø...NoÆ Marvolo said softly. ØThis is not my decision.Æ Severus inclined his head. There was no doubt in his voice. ØIf he chooses mercy,Æ Marvolo went on, Øthen the boy will be forgotten.Æ ØAnd if he does not?Æ Severus asked. Marvoloís smile this time was razor-thin. Whatever the answer .He would honour it. /// The Great Hall quieted at Minerva McGonagallís raised hand. Dinner had been midway through - plates half-full, conversation already buzzing - when the Headmistress stood from the High Table. The scrape of her chair echoed sharply, and one by one, heads turned toward her. Candles flickered overhead, casting long shadows across banners that now felt heavier with every passing week. Hogwarts felt heavier in the wake of Dumbledore's disgrace. ØI have an announcement to make,Æ Minerva said, voice carrying easily through the Hall. A hush fell. ØAs of this evening, Ronald Weasley has formally withdrawn from the Triwizard Tournament.Æ For a heartbeat, there was nothing. Any student found harassing or speculating at his expense will answer directly to me.Æ The whispers softened, but they did not stop entirely. /// At the Beauxbatons table, Fleur Delacour lowered her fork, appetite suddenly gone. Pauvre garon, she thought, a flicker of genuine sadness settling in her chest. Whatever she thought of Weasley -loud, clumsy, always scowling- he had been so young. Too young for dragons, curses, and contracts that stole magic itself. And yetè relief curled guiltily alongside the sorrow. One less champion. One less name to surpass. Missing. it would be funny if it wasn't so suspicious. Yet even with him gone the chains remained. Weasley gets out, Viktor thought, anger simmering low and hot. And I am still here. Still bound. Still expected to compete. I want it finished, he thought. I want it over. /// At the Gryffindor table, empty space marked Ron Weasleyís absence - more noticeable now than it had been all year. Minerva looked out over the Hall one final time - Looked at the faces of children who had been forced to see too much, witness things they should have been protected from. ØThat is all,Æ she said. ØYou are dismissed to continue your meal.Æ She sat. The noise slowly returned, but it had changed - tinged now with unease, with the dawning understanding that the Triwizard Tournament was not a game. And that magic, once lost, did not come back. /// The inky waters of the Black Lake churned under the winter morning sun, ripples and waves sending sparks of reflected light across the stands for the second task. The audience watching leaned forward, eyes wide with excitement and tension. Only two competitors remained - Fleur Delacour, graceful and focused, and Viktor Krum, determined and unyielding. High above the chaos, in a secluded box, Harrison and Marvolo sat side by side. Harrisonís posture was calm as he lent forward, eyes fixed on the dark water, this task was not one which drew in the audience. "I can feel your thoughts darling, tell me, did you think he would come?" Marvolo asked. Harrison hummed, he had felt off ever since the twins had told him about Rons decision, unsure though of the cause - his desire for vengeance or his desire for peace at war with one another. A part of him had not truly believe them, even with Mcgonagall announcement, but seeing the task start with only Fleur and Victor he realised that Ron truly was gone. "I think, that I expected him to try..." Marvolo reclined slightly, a faint smirk teasing his lips, watching the ripples in the water below with a predatorís patience as he though about his mates words. Harrisonís voice was quiet, casual, yet firm as he continued, ØIf Ron remains gone from the wizarding worldè Iíll let him go. No interference, no consequences beyond his own choice. to give up his magicÆ Marvoloís smirk widened, amusement glittering in his sharp eyes. ØAgain darling, I can't help but wonder if you're being too soft?Æ he teased, leaning closer. ØYou have such mercy for fools. ØIt's not soft, it's cruel really - He will live the remainder of his life as a muggle, knowing of magic, but having it just out of reach." Marvolo hummed, a dark pleasure flashing behind his eyes as he considered this. "If he removes himself from our lives then i will let him go. He can live his magicless life without fear of retaliation - Iím not going to waste my energy chasing ghosts.Æ Marvolo chuckled, the sound rich and warm, but carrying a subtle edge. And yetè still you manage to be reasonable. Itís maddening. ØDonít let it go to your head. Youíre supposed to be watching the task, notè admiring me.Æ ØI can do both,Æ Marvolo replied smoothly, his smirk returning, eyes tracking the water to see if anything was happening, Øbut in my defenceè It's not exactly a thrilling show. Harrison leaned back and laughed, "no, I suppose its not." "so, who do you think will succeed this time darling?" Marvolo asked "I honestly don't know, though last time Fleur struggled with the Grindelows" "A pity, shall we make bets on the outcome this time?" "...And what would the winner receive?" Marvolo smirked, something small and full of mischief, a rare sight, "how about a boon?" "A boon?" "Yes, something to be cashed when the winner wants." Harrison considered thing, "you're on." /// Meanwhile, the air in the infirmary was still, almost oppressively quiet, broken only by the faint hum of lingering wards. Ron sat on the edge of his bed, hands clasped loosely in his lap, staring at the faintly glowing veins along his forearms - the last traces of his magic. By not competing today it was done. He had broken the contract. At first, he had refused to believe it. This canít be happening, he thought. Magic isnít something you justè lose. The idea was absurd. He had spent his entire life surrounded by wands, spells, and enchantments. How could all of it slip through his fingers so easily? The denial clung to him like a heavy cloak, and he tried, almost desperately, to cast a simple charm. Nothing. His wand sat inert in his hand, a weightless reminder of the power that had once surged through him. A dead piece of wood. Anger followed - sudden and bitter, coiling in his chest like a serpent. He thought of Dumbledore, of the leech, of the tournament he had been thrust into. How many times had he trusted that man? How many times had he believed in his guidance, only to realise now that he had been a pawn? His jaw clenched, fists tightening, as if the act alone could somehow force some magic back into his hands. Bargaining came next, slow and hesitant, almost polite in its desperation. Maybe if I try one last spellè one more incantationè one more plea to the coreè itíll come back. Maybe if I perform perfectly in the next challengeè maybe if I just forgive himè He had made promises to himself, to his family, to lady magic herself. None of them worked. Each failure, each refusal of his wand, was another reminder that the choice had been made - and that he had made it himself. Depression followed, heavy and suffocating. He felt hollow in a way he had never imagined, as though a part of him that had always defined him had been torn away. Tears blurred his vision as he watched the faint glow in his fingertips fade, watching the veins of magic collapse into nothing. Every heartbeat echoed with the loss of potential, the loss of identity, the loss of the future he had imagined for himself as a wizard. He was small in that moment, a child again, stripped bare of everything that had once made him extraordinary. But finallyè finally came acceptance. He let the tears fall without shame, letting go of the anger, the desperation, the fantasies of what could have been. He inhaled slowly, feeling the emptiness settle into a quiet certainty. He would not be a pawn again. He would not be manipulated, coerced, or forced into battles not of his choosing. Magic had been part of his life, but it would not define him now. He was more than the spells he could cast. He was still Ron Weasley. His hands rested on his knees, the faint warmth of his last magical traces gone, leaving only the human pulse beneath his skin. He looked at the empty wand on his bed, and for the first time, he felt no bitterness. Just clarity. He had chosen. He had let go. And in that, there was freedom. For the first time ever he was free to love his life on his won terms. /// The corridors of Hogwarts felt unusually silent - Ron walked slowly, his wand tucked awkwardly in his coat pocket, a heavy weight in his step - not of exhaustion, but of finality. Bill fell in step beside him, his arm occasionally brushing against his brotherís shoulder, acting as a quiet anchor, a silent support. Arthur trailed slightly behind, his face taut with pride, sorrow, and a kind of grim acceptance not often seen on a Weasley. The air felt different to Ron now. He wasnít used to it - no magical hum in his veins, no instinctive tug toward the wand tucked in his pocket, just emptiness. The castle, so familiar once, seemed sharper somehow, more ordinary. He looked down at his hands, waiting for some spark of recognition, some flicker of the power he had carried all his life. Nothing. Just skin, veins, and the steady pulse beneath. They passed through the main hall, the light from the evening sky streaming through the tall windows, and Ron felt a quiet detachment settle over him. This was it. No spells left to cast. No charms to perform. No Triwizard Tournament to compete in. Turning a corner, they almost collided with someone coming from the opposite direction. Harrison. Harrison stiffened slightly at the sight of Ron, ready to move past, to avoid any confrontation -His of decision of peace was based on avoidance, this was not avoidance. But Ronís voice stopped him... Soft, hesitant, carrying more weight than he had expected, ØHarrison.Æ Harrison stilled, his head snapped up, surprise flickering across his features. He opened his mouth, then paused, considering leaving without comment. ØIè Iím sorry,Æ Ron said, voice low but earnest. ØI know it doesnít mean much, butè Iím sorry for everything.Æ Harrison tilted his head slightly, studying him. The apology was simple, but it carried an unfamiliar honesty. He weighed it, considered the truth behind the words, the man before him who was no longer a pawn, no longer defined by magic or mistakes. This was Ron Weasley stripped bare of everything who made him who he was. After a long pause, Harrisonís lips curved and he gave the faintest nod. ØI hope you live a good life, Ron.Æ It wasnít forgiveness, exactly. It wasnít warmth. But it was an acknowledgement. Acceptance. Enough. Ron swallowed, feeling a strange lightness, as if letting go of the weight of his magic, of his past decisions, had cleared a space in his chest for something quieter, steadier. He gave a small, almost imperceptible nod in return. Without another word, Harrison turned and walked on, leaving Ron, Bill, and Arthur to continue toward the castle exit. /// Harrison found Luna in the courtyard, sitting cross-legged on the frost-dusted grass, her gaze tracing the patterns of clouds in the winter sky, as always she seemed almost untouched by the chaos swirling inside the castle. ØHello Little Moon,Æ Harrison said softly, settling onto the stone bench beside her. Luna turned her head, offering a small, serene smile. ØHello, My Lord. You lookè thoughtful.Æ He exhaled slowly, watching the rising sun reflect across the frozen pond nearby. ØRon Weasley has left the castle. His magicè itís gone. Completely.Æ Lunaís expression softened. ØI heardè It's tragic, in a way. Losing your magicè itís like losing a part of yourself.Æ Harrison nodded, hands clasped loosely in his lap. ØIt is. And a part of meè wants to pity him. To feel sorry for him, knowing what heís lost. But the rest of meèÆ He paused, his eyes darkening for a moment. Øèthe rest of me is glad. Glad that heís been so thoroughly destroyed. Glad that heís no longer a pawn, no longer someone who could hurt others or manipulate himself into being a tool of thatè old man.Æ Luna tilted her head, studying him with calm curiosity, no judgement, ØThatís a lot to hold at once, Harrison.Æ He gave a small, almost rueful laugh. ØIt is. Thereís a part of me thatís afraid, Luna. Afraid of what I could do to him if I let my darker instincts take over. A part of me thatè wants to see him bleed. To see him pay for everything heís done.Æ ØAnd?Æ she prompted gently. Harrisonís gaze dropped to his hands. ØAnd Iím choosing not to. Iím letting him go. Iím stepping back, because if I feed that part of myselfè if I indulge itè Iíll become something I donít want to be. Letting him go means I donít have to feed the darkness. It means I stayè me.Æ Luna nodded, her voice soft but certain. ØYou were always meant for greatness, My Lord. But even greatnessè doesnít mean giving in to every shadow inside you. Choosing mercy, even when itís hard, is part of it.Æ Harrison looked at her then, a faint smile tugging at the corner of his lips. ØIím trying, Luna. Every day, Iím trying.Æ ØAnd youíre succeeding,Æ she replied simply, a quiet reassurance that carried more weight than any words of praise. Harrison exhaled again, the frostbitten air sharp in his lungs, but somehow lighter now. For the first time in days, he felt the tiniest fragment of peace. He let his gaze drift toward the castle, toward the hallways Ron had walked earlier. Letting go didnít make him weak. It made him stronger. And somehow, that was enough. /// The gates of Hogwarts loomed ahead, their ancient ironwork glinting faintly in the pale winter light. Ronís boots crunched against the frost-touched ground, each step echoing in the crisp air. Bill walked beside him, alert and protective, while Arthur stayed slightly behind, silent, letting his sons take the first steps into this new and uncertain future. The wind whipped against Ronís face, sharp and cold, and for the first time in years, he didnít reach instinctively for his wand. There was no hum of magic beneath his skin, no sense of potential waiting to be drawn. His hands felt foreign, ordinary, but in that ordinariness, there was a strange clarity. He was free from compulsion, free from Dumbledore, free from the pressure of being something he wasnít meant to be. Bill nudged him gently, ØYou alright, mate?Æ Ron didnít answer at first, letting the wind fill his lungs. He thought about the years of magic he had known, the lessons, the duels, the pride and fear - And then he thought about Dumbledoreís manipulations - the way he had used Ron as a tool, twisting loyalty into obedience and magic into a weapon. He had been angry. He had been scared. He had grieved. But nowè now it was acceptance. ØI think so,Æ he said finally, voice low. ØIímè done with it. All of it. I canít be someone elseís pawn anymore, and I canítè I can't be part of this world like I was.Æ Arthur put a hand on his shoulder, firm and grounding. ØThen you wonít be. Youíll live your life on your own terms, son. Thatís all any of us can ask.Æ Bill smiled, small and sharp, pride flashing in his eyes. ØAnd if anyone gives you troubleè Well, theyíll have to deal with me. For what itís worth, Iím glad youíre choosing this for yourself.Æ Ronís lips twitched into the faintest of smiles. It wasnít happiness exactly, not yet, but it was something, relief, maybe. A sense that he could finally breathe without expectations crushing him. The trio reached the edge of the forest bordering the school grounds and Ron paused, he couldn't help but look back at the castle one last time - The towers seemed distant now, almost unreal, bathed in frost and shadow. A place of memories, yes, but also of chains he no longer needed to bear. ØI guessè Iím really done with Hogwarts,Æ he said softly. ØDone with magic, done withè everything I thought I had to be.Æ Bill gave a small chuckle. ØYouíll find your own way, Ron. Magic or not, youíll figure it out.Æ Arthur nodded, eyes sombre but resolute, Øand weíll be here. For you. Always.Æ Ron inhaled deeply, feeling the cold, empty winter air fill him in a way he hadnít before. He didnít know what his life would look like now, what the world beyond Hogwarts would hold. But for the first time, it felt like it was entirely his to live. No compulsion. No manipulation. No expectations. /// The Room of Requirement shifted quietly around him, moulding itself to his mood as it always did. Harrison walked to the centre, the newly formed polished floor reflecting his figure, a solitary presence surrounded by the quiet hum of magic. Shadows seemed to pool along the high walls, curling around shelves lined with ancient tomes and artefacts. The air smelled faintly of parchment and candle wax, but beneath it was a tension that made the hairs on the back of his neck stand on end. He sank onto a low, velvet chair, folding his hands in his lap. The emptiness around him was a mirror for what he felt inside - still, cold, and dangerous. His thoughts drifted to Ron... to his misguided faith and the hollowing loss of magic that now defined him. A dark part of Harrison stirred, like a coiled serpent, and he felt it sliding beneath the surface. It whispered, cold and insistent -You could make him pay. You could take revenge, bend him to your will. Make him hurt for everything heís done. He clenched his fists, nails digging into his palms, as he forced his mind away. The voice was tempting, a sirens call that promised satisfaction... But he had walked too far, seen too much, to let it consume him. He thought of Marvoloís teasing earlier -Too soft- If only that was the truth. No, he told the shadow. I am not that person. I will not feed you. And yet, even as he made the choice, a part of him acknowledged the thrill he might have felt if he had given in. The power to destroy, the absolute control, the satisfaction of seeing someone like Ron broken - there was a pulse in that darkness, a sharp, electric pull. Harrison exhaled slowly, leaning back in the chair and letting the shadows swirl around him without touching him. Choosing to let Ron go, to allow him to live a life without magic, was a strange kind of victory. Not for the world, not even for Ron, but for the part of Harrison that refused to become a tyrant of his own impulses. He closed his eyes, feeling the pulse of the Room beneath him, the quiet heartbeat of the magic that had always responded to him. He was strong, more than he had ever been in his old life, and yet, he still didn't feel like it was enough. Still he reminded himself, strength was not measured in destruction. It was measured in restraint. /// The sunlight burned through the hospital window, harsh and unrelenting, though it didnít touch her the way it once had... Molly sat upright in her bed, hands shaking as she tried to grasp her wand, only to find it inert in her fingers. The soft hum of magic she had relied on her entire life was gone, leaving a deafening silence in its place. a hollow void which seemed to grow with each day. She had done the same thing for weeks. Unable to comprehend what was gone. What she was. Her eyes widened in disbelief. ØNoè no, no, no!Æ she whispered, voice hoarse and cracking. She waved the wand again, muttering incantations she had cast a thousand times -but nothing- The spells, the charms, the familiar currents of magic that had always been hersè all gone. Mollyís heart pounded, a cocktail of panic, rage, and betrayal. She clawed at the sheets, staring at her hands as though they had betrayed her. They were justè hands. Ordinary, useless, powerless. ØItís himè itís Dumbledore!Æ she screamed, tears streaking her face. ØHeís done this! Itís all his fault! Ronè my Ronè and meè weíve beenè used! Used!Æ Her voice cracked with hysteria, and she lurched forward, trying to summon the strength she once had. Nothing. Her body and mind fought against the absence he so keenly felt, her identity unravelling with its loss. Her magic - her very essence - had been the measure of who she was, and now it was gone. She slammed her fists on the bed, then wrapped herself tightly in the covers, shaking. ØHowè how could Iè how could I let it happen?Æ she whispered, voice small now, trembling. Every memory of obedience, loyalty, and trust in Dumbledore burned like acid in her mind. Every spell, every lesson, every flicker of power - it had all been a lie. And worst of allè Ron. Her son. He had chosen to give up his magic, to step out of the life she had always wanted for him. She had failed him, failed herself, failed them both. Rage and grief twisted inside her, but it was punctuated by a hollow, bitter awareness - they were free from her manipulations, free from Dumbledoreís schemes, and sheè she had nothing left. Her breaths came in ragged, shallow gasps as she stared at the ceiling, the empty air around her. The reality pressed down like a physical weight: she was a squib now. Ronís choice had been his own, and yet somehow, it had highlighted her own failure, her own undoing. ØBetrayedè betrayedè betrayed!Æ she wailed, curling in on herself. ØItís allè his fault! All of it!Æ And in that scream, in that raw, frantic panic, the first seed of clarity began to take root. She was powerless. And it was all the fault, this was the costè the cost of her manipulations - she had lost everything that had defined herè this realisation would linger, unrelenting. /// Amelia Bones entered the hospital room quietly, her robes crisp and wand tucked neatly at her side. The faint beeping of monitoring machines punctuated the heavy air, and Molly - her hair dishevelled, eyes bloodshot, and body frail - lay propped up in bed, still recovering from the magical destabilisation that had stripped her of her powers. ØMrsè No-Name,Æ Amelia began, her voice calm but firm, carrying the authority of the DMLE even thpugh she was exhaused, ØIíve come to speak with you regarding your involvement with Albus Dumbledore.Æ Mollyís head lolled weakly to the side, a laugh that didnít reach her eyes escaping her lips. ØInvolvement?Æ she whispered, voice trembling. ØIíve already lost everythingè my magicè my familyè my sonè havenít I suffered enough?Æ Amelia pulled a chair up to the bed, sitting down with the weight of the law pressing in her very soul. ØYes. You have suffered. Losing your magic, the consequences of Dumbledoreís compulsions, the loss of your sonís magical path - all of that is very real. But your suffering does not absolve you of responsibility for your actions. You aided him. You knowingly facilitated manipulations that endangered countless lives.Æ Mollyís hands shook violently as she gripped the sheets. ØI didnít knowè I couldnítè it was allè Dumbledore! Havenít I paid enough?Æ Her voice rose, cracking under the strain. ØYou want me to face trial on top of this? After all Iíve lost?Æ ØYes,Æ Amelia said, her tone measured, unyielding. ØYour suffering is the consequence of your choices - but it does not erase them. The law requires accountability. You will face trial. You will answer for the laws you have broken.Æ Mollyís body sagged back against the pillows, a bitter laugh escaping. ØSo losing my magicè losing Ginnyè Ronè thatís not enough for you?Æ Ameliaís gaze softened slightly, though the authority never wavered. ØIt is a consequence, Mrs. No-Name. But consequences alone are not justice. You will have the opportunity to speak, to explain your actions, and the court will decide the severity of your sentence. The fact remains: your complicity in Dumbledoreís crimes cannot be ignored.Æ Mollyís hands fell to her lap, trembling as tears welled in her eyes. ØIíve already lost everythingè I canít lose any moreèÆ ØYes,Æ Amelia said quietly, standing. ØAnd now you will answer for what remains.Æ She gave one last unwavering look at Molly before turning and leaving the room. The soft click of the door echoed against the sterile walls, leaving Molly alone, fragile, powerless - and acutely aware that the law would not spare her suffering. /// The kitchen of the Burrow was warmer than the winter air outside, but the atmosphere inside was heavy, almost suffocating. Arthur sat at the head of the table, his hands wrapped around a cup of tea that had long since gone cold. Bill leaned forward, elbows on the table, while Charlie and Percy sat opposite, faces grim. ØIíve received the notice this morning,Æ Arthur began, voice tight. ØMolly will be going to trial. Sheíll answer for her actions and involvement with Dumbledoreís schemes.Æ Bill rubbed the bridge of his nose, exhaling slowly. ØI canít say Iím surprised, butè itís still hard to hear. Sheísè well, she was your wife - our mom. Itís not easy, Dad.Æ Arthurís jaw tightened. ØIíve long since accepted whatís happened, William. She chose her path. And actions have consequences, she may have paid for some of them already but not all... she must answer for the rest.Æ Percy shifted uncomfortably in his chair, clearly torn between loyalty and duty. ØAnd Ronè what are we going to do about him? Heís adjusting to beingè without magic... but heís not exactlyè thriving, is he?Æ Charlie gave a short, humourless laugh. ØThriving? Heís not even eating properly half the time. The boy's struggling with the loss of his identity, with finding new purpose. Magic was everything to him.Æ Billís eyes softened. ØHeíll get there. He just needs time, supportè and the knowledge that weíre behind him, no matter what. Heís not a wizard anymore, but heís still our brother, and he still needs us.Æ Arthur nodded. ØIíve spoken to him, and heís trying.Æ Percy frowned. ØStill to just accept it, to willingly become a squibè thatís a hard road... Will he ever forgive Dumbledore?Æ Arthurís eyes darkened slightly. ØI doubt it. But forgiveness isnít the point here. Survival is. Heís chosen life without magic over life under manipulation. Thatís the choice we support.Æ Charlie leaned back, arms crossed. ØAnd weíll make sure heís occupied. Heíll find something to be good at, something that isnít dependent on magic. He always has a head for strategies.Æ Bill nodded. ØWe can start small. Skills, hobbiesè maybe doing some work around the Burrow? Heíll adjust eventually. But he must know itís not a punishment, itís his choice.Æ Percyís tone softened. ØIt justè feels strange. Seeing him so different. And with Mum on trial nowè the familyís been through so much.Æ Arthur let out a heavy sigh, glancing out the window at the snow-covered garden. ØStrange, yes. But necessary. We protect the ones we love, even when itís painful. Ron made a choice, Molly made hers. Weíll manage the aftermath as best we can.Æ Charlie shook his head. ØStill, itís going to be hard to look at him and not think about what heís lost.Æ Bill reached over, placing a hand on Charlieís shoulder. ØYes. But weíll remind him what he still has - family, love, and a life thatís his own. Thatís more than enough.Æ. Chapter End Notes Thanks to my friend who has Beta'd this chapter for me, there should hopefully be no major mistakes. Sit down before it gets any colder.Æ ØIím not hungry,Æ Ron muttered, though his voice lacked the fire it had once carried. You may not have magic, but you still have yourself.Æ Ron sank into a chair, burying his face in his hands.
