Not power. But possibility. Life, maybe, even without the world he had once known. /// The hospital room felt impossibly small, the pale walls and sterile light doing nothing to soften the dread pressing down on Molly. She sat upright in her bed, still weak, her body unsteady after the magical stripping of her powers. The door burst open before she could even gather her thoughts. ØMolly No-name, youíre to come with us,Æ an Auror barked, his wand already raised. His face was hard, his tone lacking any semblance of sympathy. ØIím ill,Æ Molly croaked, her voice weak and wavering. ØI canít-please, I need-Æ ØNo excuses. The Ministry has ordered your transfer. Youíll answer for your crimes,Æ he cut her off. Two other Aurors stepped in, looming over her like shadows. One of them, taller and broader, with a permanent sneer etched across his face, muttered under his breath loud enough for her to hear, ØSquibs like you shouldnít even be here. Take up space somewhere else, not the wizarding world.Æ Molly froze, the words slicing through her fragile composure. The others ignored him, but the venom in his tone seemed to linger in the room like smoke. She had no magic to defend herself. They guided her weakly through the halls, past the bustling aurors and wizards of the Ministry. Each step felt heavier than the last, her body trembling with exhaustion and fear. By the time they arrived at the holding cells, her legs barely supported her. ØCell 17. Make it secure,Æ the sneering Auror said, shoving her inside. The door clanged shut behind her. The finality, the silence of it, echoing. Molly collapsed onto the cold stone floor, curling into herself. The dim light from the small barred window offered no comfort. She could hear the distant voices of other prisoners .jeering, screaming, pleading. Her hands shook as she pressed them against her face, trying to block out the world. ØIè Iímè nothing,Æ she whispered, the words catching in her throat. ØIíve lost everythingè my magic, my familyè and for what? For him? Dumbledoreè itís all his faultèÆ The sobs came in ragged bursts, echoing off the stone walls. She clawed at the floor as though trying to hold onto something -anything- real, tangible, that could anchor her to her past. Hours passed in a blur. She barely noticed when an Auror passed by, his eyes flicking down at her with a mixture of annoyance and disgust. ØSquibs,Æ he muttered, shaking his head, Øalways the most trouble, always the weakest.Æ She wanted to scream at him, to defend herself, to fight - but her voice had no power, her body no strength. Magic had been her life, her identity, her shield. Without it, she felt hollow. /// Albus sat alone in the shadowed corner of his study, the flickering candlelight casting long, wavering shadows across the walls. His hands were folded in his lap, his mind unusually still as the news settled in. The decrepit manor he had claimed for himself was a long way from the majesty he was used to. Its mere existence was an insult to him. ØRonald Weasley,Æ he muttered under his breath, tasting the words like a bitter draught. He had expected more - expected courage, defiance, loyalty. Instead, the boy had chosenè surrender. To abandon magic. To abandon the fight. A pang of irritation, edged with disbelief, cut through him. Coward. That was the word he kept repeating in his mind. Coward. He should have risen, he should have wanted to fight for the Light, for the order he had tried to instil in him. The boy had been given the chance to step into history, to carve his place, and he had fled into nothingness. What a disappointment. Albus leaned back, eyes narrowing. The thought of it grated against him- Weasley refusing to play his part. It was worse than mere cowardice. It was failure. A miscalculation. He had counted on Ron, or at least on the potential he represented, and the boy had chosen to walk away. And Mollyè Molly Weasley. Weak. Pathetic. Even after everything she had suffered, she had not resisted. Not fought. Not defied. She had simply let the Ministry take her away without so much as a fight, to crumble so easilyè She had been a pawn in his game, and she had failed to move as he intended. Weak. Chess pieces. That was how he saw them now. All of them. Pawns and knights and bishops, each with their place, each with a purpose. And yet here were two pieces, a mother and son, that he had lost entirely. They would never play the role he had envisioned for them. They were off the board, and it frustrated him to no end. Albus rose, pacing the room slowly, his robes brushing the floor in a soft whisper. A part of him considered sending a message, a warning to Ron, perhaps a reminder of what might have been. But no. he decided, I will let him go. Let him feel the weight of his own weakness. Let him live with the knowledge that he had refused the life he was offered. Let him realise his mistakes. Mollyís absence, her arrestè was another irritation, a reminder of the pieces he had misjudged. He clenched his jaw, anger simmering behind a calm facade. They had been part of his game, and now they were gone, pawns lost to the tide of their own choices. He was losing, and it was their fault. Albus exhaled slowly, leaning over his desk. His hands hovered over the parchment, but he did not write. Plans, contingencies, strategies - they would continue. The board was still vast, still full of pieces to command. But these twoè they were the first pawns he had miscalculated in this new game. And he would not forget. Nor would he forgive. ØCowards,Æ he whispered, almost with disdainful pity. ØBoth of them. Weak, frightened, insignificant. And yetè even in their loss, they have taught me something. The game continues.Æ His plans for the boy would go ahead. He refused to lose to the dark. To Tom. He may be losing now, but he would win. The third task, with its labyrinthine like maze and shifting dangers, would be the perfect opportunity. If he could isolate Harrison, if he could snatch him away while the world was distracted, then the boy would be entirely at his mercy. He could teach him. Shape him. Force him to see the world the way he did. And if not.. then he could remove him. Albusís lips curved into a faint, almost imperceptible smile. ØAnd the others,Æ he murmured to himself, Øwould never see it coming.Æ The thought pleased him - Harrisonís friends, his allies, his blasted traitor of a father, all so confident in their little circle, oblivious to the danger creeping closer. He imagined the moment: Harrisonís surprise, the flash of defiance in his eyes, the tiny struggle before he realised resistance was useless. And thenè the control, the dominance. The boy would understand, eventually. Or he would break. Either way, he would be gone from the board. Albusís gaze sharpened. He pictured the exact moment to strike, every distraction in place, every outcome calculated. Nothing could go wrong. Not if he timed it perfectly. The task itself would shield him from prying eyes; the chaos, the excitement, the fear - it would be the perfect cover. Yetè a flicker of hesitation passed through him, so faint he barely noticed it. Harrison wasnít like the others. Clever, intuitive, powerful in ways Albus had only begun to understand. Perhaps the boy would resist more than expected. Perhaps the boy could evenè escape. No. He shook the thought away. No. Harrison was strong, yes - but he was still a pawn, a piece on the board, and Albus was the master player. Every risk had a counter. Every move had been anticipated. He would win. /// The library was quiet, the usual hum of parchment and whispered conversation absent. Harrison sat at a long, oak table with his friends, his coven, scattered around him, all working on homework which felt pointless in the grand scheme of things. ØSo Fleur and Victor completed the second taskè is it just me or did it feel anti-climactic?Æ Hannah asked, frowning as she set her book down. ØYeah,Æ Harrison replied, voice low, almost detached. ØWith everything else going onè the tournament barely registers anymore.Æ He let his gaze drift to the parchment before him, tracing the numbers with a finger. ØVictorís in the lead with 83 points now, and Fleurís got 77.Æ Neville leaned forward, his brow furrowed. ØYouíve been quietè are you okay?Æ Harrison gave a faint shrug, trying for casualness, but there was weight in his eyes. ØIím fine. Justè bracing for the next hurdle.Æ His friends exchanged glances, Pansy letting out a small laugh. ØAlways thinking ahead, arenít you?Æ Luna tilted her head, her pale eyes luminous in the sunlight. ØThe hurdles arenít always the ones you expect. Sometimes the ground shifts before the obstacle appears.Æ Harrison paused, considering her words. A shiver ran down his spine - not from fear, but from recognition. Something was coming, something different from what had happened last time. He could feel it, like a tremor under the surface, subtle but insistent. ØThe third task,Æ he murmured under his breath, a faint smile tugging at his lips. ØI wonder what that will bring.Æ Neville gave a small nod, unconvinced but trusting. ØWellè weíll be here with you.Æ Harrison looked around at his friends, his coven, the bond they shared, the quiet trust in their eyes. He felt the warmth of it, a grounding amidst everything else, and let himself sit a little straighter. He was their lord, they had chosen him, and he wouldn't fail them. ØYeah,Æ he said finally. ØAnd Iíll need every bit of it. This next oneè itís going to be different.Æ Lunaís smile was enigmatic, almost knowing. ØDifferentè yes. But youíve always been destined for change, havenít you?Æ Harrison stared at her, the cryptic weight of her words settling in. ØNot going to give me a hint, little Moon?Æ Luna smiled wanly, Øangry bees tend to strike without thinking of the consequences, but remember, they only get one chance.Æ Harrison nodded, Øyouíre a gift.Æ Luna preaned, the weight in her gaze lifting, Øbraid my hair?Æ she asked. Harrison nodded and held out a hand to draw her in - He knew that this was a distraction .that this was Luna's way of helping him to relax, to ground him, and he let her. With his hands buried in her hair, he allowed himself a moment to simply bask in this small bubble of peace, he knew it would not last. But for now, he would cherish it. /// The courtroom was packed, the air heavy with anticipation. Aurors lined the walls, their wands at the ready, while journalists scribbled notes furiously. Rita Skeeter was practically vibrating with tension, quills poised ready to strike like blades on the floating parchment. She would not miss even a second of this trial. Bill and Arthur sat in the stands, side by side, their faces pale under the dim enchanted lighting. Neither spoke, the silence between them tense, a mixture of dread and disbelief hanging thick in the air. Molly No-Name- as the court now referred to her, sat at the defendantís table. Her hair was unkempt, her robes wrinkled, and her eyes hollowed from weeks in hospital followed by her ministry confinement. She did not meet their gaze. Her hands were clasped tightly in front of her, fingers trembling. The prosecutor laid out her involvement with Dumbledore, detailing the compulsions she had helped cast, the magical manipulations, the ways she had knowingly assisted in his schemes. Each accusation landed like a hammer, echoing through the cavernous courtroom. Rita was horrified, this was fantastic. Arthur swallowed hard. He could still see the woman he had loved, the mother of his children, and yet here she was, reduced to a pawn in Dumbledoreís twisted game. His chest ached - not just for her, but for the betrayal, for the consequences of her choices. He refused to allow himself to crumble though. He thought of Ron. He thought of Ginny. Billís hands clenched tightly in his lap. His mother. The woman who had raised him, loved him, taught him right from wrong. Watching her now, stripped of her dignity, sentenced to Azkaban, was unbearable in a way he hadnít imagined. Part of him wanted to scream at the injustice of it, and part of himè part of him felt the vindication of seeing someone who had made choices so recklessly finally be held accountable. The judgeís gavel slammed down, shaking the courtroom. ØMolly No-Name - you are hereby sentenced to Azkaban for aiding and abetting the crimes of Albus Dumbledore and for the unlawful use of compulsive magic.Æ Mollyís lips trembled. She opened her mouth as if to speak, but no sound came. She simply bowed her head, the weight of her lifeís mistakes pressing her into herself. Arthur exhaled shakily, trying to hold himself together. ØItísè itís over,Æ he whispered to Bill, though his own heart felt far from relief. Bill shook his head slightly, his jaw tight. ØYeahè itís over,Æ he echoed, though he felt hollow. He didnít know whether to mourn the mother he had loved or the woman who had betrayed so many lives. The guards moved forward, chains appearing around Mollyís wrists. She stood slowly, shuffling forward with a haunted, almost resigned gait. Bill and Arthur watched her go, each step a reminder of the fracture in their family, the impossibility of reconciling love with justice. Arthurís hand tightened on Billís shoulder briefly - a silent acknowledgement of shared grief. ØWe will get through this,Æ he murmured, though even he wasnít certain if that was true. Bill only nodded, his eyes following his mother as she disappeared through the heavy doors of the courtroom, leaving only the echo of her choices behind. /// Ron sat on the edge of his small, spartan room at the Burrow, the late winter light filtering through the curtains. The house felt unusually quiet, as if it, too, were holding its breath. Without his magic, even simple movements felt heavier, more deliberate - but he had grown used to the weight of the mundane. His father and Bill had told him the news earlier, blunt and without ceremony. His mother had been sentenced to Azkaban. Molly Weasley -now Molly No-name- once a figure of warmth and power in his life, was now removed from the world of magic entirely. Ron didnít cry. He didnít even feel the anger he might have expected. Relief had taken its place. Relief that the woman who had so recklessly manipulated and endangered him and others, following Dumbledore without question, had finally been held accountable. But relief was tangled with grief, sharper and colder. Ginny. He thought of her often now, of the explosion that had killed her... He still held doubts about Harrison's innocence, but a small part of him knew that either way, Harrison wasn't the true cause of her death. No, that honour lay with their mother, with Dumbledore. Their twisted schemes and manipulations had taken his sister's life. Had taken the life he thought he was going to live. Ron pressed his hands to his lap, staring at them, ordinary and powerless now. He had chosen his path - he had given up magic to remain free of manipulation - but the freedom was bittersweet. The quiet of the room pressed in on him, but he let it. He wasnít crying. He wasnít screaming. He simply breathed, feeling the emptiness and the relief coexist in a way that made him feel strangely steady. He had made his choice. /// Sirius swore as he walked toward the door of Grimmauld place. ØSomebody had better be dying,Æ he muttered under his breath as he swung the door open .narrowly avoiding being punched in the face by a frantic Tonks. Tonks practically stumbled in, eyes wide, her metamorphmagus hair shifting nervously between shades of silver and violet. ØSirius,Æ she gasped, voice low and urgent, Øyou need to listen to me. Now.Æ Sirius raised an eyebrow, before leading her into the house ØSlow down, Tonks. Breathe. Whatís wrong?Æ She shook her head, pacing, as though the movement could burn the tension out of her. ØItísè itís Albus. Dumbledore. Heís planning something. Somethingè awful.Æ Sirius leaned forward, frowning. ØPlanning what? Tonks, youíre scaring me half to death already.Æ ØI didnít mean to-Æ Tonks stopped, swallowing hard. ØI didnít mean to fall in with him. I - I thought I could helpè or at least not make things worse. But Iím not loyal to him. I swear, Sirius, I never wanted to help him. I never wanted this.Æ Sirius softened slightly at her desperation but kept his gaze sharp. She was family, but Harrison was his son, ØThen tell me exactly whatís happening. Donít hold back.Æ Tonks nodded, voice trembling, but she forced herself to speak. ØHeè heís planning to kidnap Harrison. During the Third Task. He knows Harrison wonít be competing - heíll just be watchingîbutè heís going to try to take him while everyoneís distracted.Æ Siriusís jaw tightened, his hand clenching the edge of his desk. ØThe Third Task? Dumbledore thinks he can just - ?Æ ØYes,Æ Tonks interrupted, stepping closer. ØHe thinks he can take him. He wants Harrison out of the wayè maybe to force him to fight for him, maybe as leverage. I donít know exactly. But I swear, Sirius, Iím not in this. I never wanted to help him. I justè I didnít know how to stop it before. But nowè Iím telling you. You have to do something.Æ Sirius ran a hand through his hair, his silver eyes flashing with that dangerous mix of worry and controlled fury. ØAlright. You did the right thing by coming to me. Iíll handle this. Donít let him see you acting scared - heíll know. Weíll need a plan before the Third Task starts.Æ Tonks nodded quickly, relief flickering over her face, though worry still lingered in her eyes. ØI - I just didnít want anyone to get hurt. Not Harrison. Not anyone. PleaseèÆ Sirius stood and walked over, placing a reassuring hand on her shoulder. ØYou did the right thing, Tonks. Now weíll make sure Dumbledore doesnít get what he wants.Æ Tonks exhaled, a shaky but genuine breath this time, and for a moment, the chaos in her mind calmed. But even as she looked at Sirius, she couldnít shake the thought of Harrison, completely unaware, and the storm about to come for him. After she left Sirius let out a breath, he knew Albus wouldn't go down easy, but to target his pup again? Well, he would show that old bastard just why the Blacks were once the most feared family in Europe. /// Sirius did not bother with pretence when he entered The Dark Lord's study. The wards parted for him without protest, recognising him as Lord Black, and the heavy door shut behind him with a sound like a verdict being passed. He knew the amount of leniency he had with the Dark Lord was based purely on Harrison. The Dark Lord looked up from the book he had been reading, dark eyes immediately sharpening. ØYou donít arrive like that unless something has gone wrong,Æ he pointed out calmly. Sirius folded his arms. ØTonks came to me. Sheís shaken - but sheís telling the truth. Dumbledoreís planning to kidnap Harrison during the Third Task. Heís banking on the distraction of the tasks making him vulnerable." The temperature in the room dropped. Marvolo did not move. He did not raise his voice. He simply closed the book with deliberate care and set it aside, fingers lingering on the cover as though restraining something far more violent than ink and parchment. ØI see,Æ he said softly. Sirius shifted, uneasy despite himself. ØI wanted to know what you plan to do. Because if he tries-Æ ØI will wait,Æ Marvolo interrupted. Sirius blinked. ØYouíllè wait?Æ ØYes.Æ Marvolo rose to his full height, magic rolling off him in slow, controlled waves. ØHarrison is not a child to be hidden behind my will. He is my soulmate, my other half, my equal. I respect him too much to make decisions about his fate without him.Æ There was no softness in his words, but there was something far deeper: certainty. Sirius studied him carefully. ØYouíre really going to let him choose.Æ Marvoloís mouth curved faintly. ØAlways.Æ Then his expression hardened, the calm cracking just enough for Sirius to glimpse what lay beneath. ØBut,Æ Marvolo continued, voice dropping into something lethal, Øif Albus Dumbledore so much as touches Harrison - if he reaches for him with spell, hand, or intent - I will destroy everything he has ever loved.Æ The wards trembled. ØEvery ideal. Every follower. Every legacy he believes will outlive him,Æ Marvolo said evenly, as though discussing the latest quidditch scores. ØI will unravel him until there is nothing left but a frightened old man who finally understands what it means to lose.Æ Sirius swallowed, nodding once. ØGood to know.Æ It was times like this that he was reminded who this man was, the destruction he had once caused, ØIíll keep eyes on Dumbledore until then, make sure Tonks keeps me in the loop.Æ ØGood,Æ Marvolo replied, already turning back toward the window that overlooked the grounds. ØLet him plan. Let him hope.Æ His gaze went distant, ØSome lessons,Æ Marvolo said quietly, Øcan only be learned when the board believes it is winning.Æ And Sirius knew, with absolute certainty, that if Dumbledore made his move, the world would burn for it. And, there was a part of him that relished it that thought. In the dark promise the lord before him made, as he knew that even if it meant burning the world down, Harrison would be protected. ØYouíre terrifying, do you know that?Æ /// The office was silent except for the scratching of quills and the occasional shuffle of parchment. Amelia sat hunched over her desk, stacks of reports and notebooks surrounding her like a small fortress. Candlelight flickered against the high windows, casting elongated shadows that made the room feel both vast and suffocating. Her eyes, sharp and unrelenting, scanned a report for the third time, ink smudged from constant reference. Every line, every note from aurors, every trace of magical residue was catalogued meticulously in her mind. Dumbledore had always been careful, but carefulness left patterns if one looked closely enough. She just had to find them. ØThe escape, the attacks, the compulsionsèÆ she muttered to herself, tracing her finger over an older report. ØThere has to be a rhythmè a signature. Something consistent. Something he has left behind.Æ She picked up a small vial containing a sample of residual magic taken from one of the wards heíd manipulated. Even in the aftermath of his departure, the trace of his magic was faint but distinctive. Amelia swirled it under her nose, noting the peculiar undertones -an alchemy of light and dark, intertwined, impossible to classify neatly. Her hands trembled slightly as she jotted down another note. She had been awake for nearly twenty-four hours straight, and yet the exhaustion felt secondary to the drive fuelling her actions. Every day Dumbledore remained at large was another day more lives could be manipulated, corrupted, or destroyed. A faint knock at the door made her pause, quill hovering midair. ØEnter,Æ she said, not looking up. The door opened slowly, and a junior auror poked their head in. ØMadam Bones the latest reports from the school-Æ Amelia waved them in without breaking her focus. ØGood. Place them here.Æ She tapped a corner of her desk, and the auror laid the papers down quietly, almost afraid to disturb her. Amelia barely glanced at them, already flipping through the stack of old correspondences, cross-referencing, connecting dots in her mind. She leaned back in her chair for a moment, running a hand over her face. ØHeís playing a game, yesè but even the greatest chess masters leave the faintest tell. And I will see it. I will find him.Æ Outside the window, the wind rattled the panes, carrying the distant echoes of students on the grounds. Amelia didnít notice. Her world had narrowed to lines of text, swirls of magic, and a singular, immovable objective: Albus Dumbledore had to be stopped. And somewhere in her meticulous planning, she allowed herself the smallest, almost imperceptible smile. The chase was on, and she had no intention of losing. /// Harrison was sitting in the library when the owl arrived, swooping through the window with a flap of wings and a small, heavy envelope clutched in its talons. He caught it effortlessly, noting the dark seal and sharp edges that immediately spoke of its sender. Breaking the seal, he unfolded the letter. Harrison, Meet me in Hogsmeade tomorrow, midday, by the Three Broomsticks. We need to speak about Dumbledore. It concerns you directly. -Marvolo Harrisonís brow lifted, curiosity sparking in his green eyes. Marvolo didn't write to him often, at least not letters which sounded like this - Curiosity filled him as well as a sense of dreadè why would Marvolo write to him, what had happened? ØWhat owl is brave enough to risk madam Pince?Æ Blaise asked, eyeing the owl as if it was something demonic. ØOne owned by my soul mate.Æ ØAh, that would do it I suppose.Æ Harrison huffed a laugh as he wrote a confirmation. It was the weekend tomorrow, and even if it wasn't a Hogsmead one, getting out of the castle unnoticed wasn't a great hardship. ØAnything interesting?Æ Neville asked, ever curious. Harrison hummed, Øhe wants to meet me tomorrow, at the Three Broomsticks.Æ ØDo you think something has happened?Æ Hannah asked. Harrison tilted his head, Øprobably, but what, I'm not sure.Æ /// The following day, Harrison stepped out into the crisp winter air of Hogsmeade, cloaked and alert. By midday, he spotted Marvolo leaning against a lamppost, the collar of his coat turned up against the wind, eyes scanning the street until they locked with Harrisonís. A small smile tugged at Marvoloís lips as Harrison approached. ØYou look too casual for someone sneaking out of school for te first timeÆ Marvolo said, voice low, almost teasing. ØCasual works better when you're sneaking around, surely somebody with yourè qualificationsè should know this,Æ Harrison responded, "and who said this is my first time?" Marvolo let out a breath a small smirk tugging at his lips before leading them into a humble looking home, causing Harrison to raise a brow in question. ØIt belongs to one of my followers,Æ He explained the unasked question. ØConvenient.Æ Marvolo hummed and moved to sit by the window. Harrison could see the lines of tension in his frame, feel the magic under his skin practically begging to get out. ØWhat has happened?Æ Marvoloís eyes darkened, ØDumbledore is planning to take you.Æ ØTake me? He wants to kidnap me?Æ ØYes, while the Third Task is happening, he will move. He wants to use the distraction of the tournament and strike where he knows youíll be least protected.Æ Harrison raised an eyebrow. Considering this new information. ØI seeè maybe I should just let him take me?Æ he said lightly, a small smirk playing on his lips. ØWouldnít that be poetic? The old goat finally ending himself, using me as the catalyst.Æ Marvoloís jaw tightened. ØNo,Æ he said sharply, voice low and dangerous. ØAbsolutely not. You are not to be touched, not by him, not by anyone. Do you hear me?Æ There was a sharpness in his voice that most would akin to anger, but Harrison could feel the anguish in the words, could feel how Marvoloís magic practically smothered him, as if doing so would protect him and keep him safe from harm. ØI hear you,Æ Harrison replied, voice soft with acknowledgement, Øbut think about itè itís perfect. One move, and he falls. All his followers will crumble and the world will see him for what he is, there will be no denying it anymore, not even his most loyal little sycophants will be able to defend him.Æ ØAnd if he harms you?Æ marvolo demanded. ØYou know I cannot die,Æ Harrison reminded him. ØDeath is not my concern, death is a mercy. They could hurt you, Harrison.Æ Harrison paused, considering this, Øthey could,Æ he acknowledged, Øbut, do you truly believe I would let them? I am not weak.Æ ØThis isn't about weakness, this is about willingly walking into danger.Æ ØDanger, maybe. But truly I think it will be worth it, this will end it all, my love. We will be able to bring Dumbledore to his knees.Æ Marvoloís hand clenched at his side, eyes blazing. ØYou are not a pawn in his game! I will not let you risk yourself like that. You are mine, Harrison, and I will protect you - even if I have to burn every bridge he has ever walked.Æ Harrison tilted his head, eyes glinting with challenge. ØAnd yet, it could be the cleanest way to end him. You know it. We could watch the whole house of cards fall, then step in. No collateral damage - he will be the only piece lost.Æ Marvoloís lips pressed into a hard line. He considered the suggestion for a long moment, dark fury fighting with reason. Finally, he looked at his soul mate, his little raven looked absolute. Seeing this he exhaled, a grudging concession in his voice. ØFine,Æ he said, Øbut you will not walk into this without protection. This isn't a game and you're not a pawn. If you step into his clutches it will be on my terms.Æ Harrison smirked, the tension in his shoulders loosening. ØConcessions. I like it. Youíre learning to negotiate, darling.Æ Marvolo grunted, brushing a hand through his hair, lips twitching in the faintest hint of a smile. ØYou're beautiful when youíre powerful, but you look devilish when you scheme,Æ he muttered, voice low. ØBut don't forget, that if you dare let anything foolish happen, I will ensure he regrets the day he even thought of touching you.Æ ØNoted,Æ Harrison said, a glimmer of excitement in his eyes. ØBut trust me, Marvolo, itís going to be fine. In fact, it will be glorious to watch the old man finally crumble, to finally be free of him.Æ Marvoloís gaze darkened again, half amusement, half warning. ØGlorious, perhaps. But I will be watching. Every step. And If he so much as bruises you, everything he has ever loved dies.Æ Harrison nodded once, eyes sharp. ØThen I'll aim to be bruise free.Æ /// Harrison returned to Hogwarts under the grey winter sky, the weight of the day pressing on him even as he walked through the familiar halls. The castle seemed quieter than usual, the echoes of footsteps and laughter muted by the shadow of the coming Third Task. He was ready for this to be over. Not just the tournament but Dumbledore's schemes. He would never regret coming back, having this opportunity to be with Marvolo, to truly live his life, but at times he found himself exhausted by the weight of it all. He wanted to be free, to just be able to live. In the Room of Requirement, he began making some preparations. Wards bloomed from his fingertips, shimmering like silver threads in the dim light, weaving a lattice of protection around him. Emergency portkeys hovered in the air, invisible until called, each keyed to a different safe location - safe even from the most ingenious of Dumbledoreís spells. He may be willing to offer himself up as bait, but he wouldn't do so without preparation. He knew - even without Marvolo's threat of destruction - that if anything happened to him that it would be the people he loved that truly suffered. Marvolo had tried to hide it behind a mask of anger, but his fear had bled through the link clearly. Harrison might be willing to risk himself,, but he would'r risk them - their peace of mind, their happiness. /// Neville noticed the change in him ØYouíre quiet today,Æ Neville said softly. ØEverything okay?Æ Harrison paused, glancing at his friend. ØIím fine,Æ he said, voice steady, though his mind churned. ØJustè bracing for the next hurdle.Æ Hannah and Susan had settled nearby, quietly observing, Luna was off in the corner, her eyes distant and thoughtful. Harrison thought about Dumbledore - how clever, how patient, and how utterly ruthless the old man could be. And yet, he felt a thrill run through him. This was the culmination of months of strategy, power, and preparation. Every step Dumbledore thought heíd controlled would be anticipated, and finally the man's reign would be over. /// Gathering his coven in the room of requirement and letting them know the plan was both easier and harder than he had anticipated. ØHave you lost your mind?Æ Draco asked, aghast. ØMaybe taken a bludger to the head-Æ Ø-Maybe needing one?Æ the twins commented. Harrison sighed, ØI know itís risky-Æ Ø-Risky- No, risky is sneaking into Hogsmeade to meet up with your soulmate. Keep me grounded,Æ Harrison told them. He didn't need help with the technical aspects, but rather, keeping himself in check, as a darker part of him thrummed with impatience, a hunger for confrontation. Let him try, he thought. Youíll know it when it does. But storms have a way of clearing the airè and revealing whatís true.Æ Harrison tilted his head, letting her words sink in. She always seemed to see the edges of what was coming, the currents beneath the surface. He allowed himself a small smile, a mixture of respect and amusement. ØIíll be ready,Æ he said, more to himself than to anyone else. ØFor him, for everything he thinks he can do. For a brief moment, he allowed himself to simply feel power .controlled, restrained, ready. He was no longer just a watcher. He was the storm waiting to strike, and Dumbledoreís arrogance would be his undoing. /// The morning sun streamed through the tall windows of the Great Hall, catching the frost that lingered on the glass and painting the long tables in gold and silver light. Harrison sat at the Ravenclaw table, Luna beside him, her eyes drifting dreamily over the room, as if she were already halfway into another world. The tables were alive with chatter, but there was an undercurrent of tension - the Third Task loomed, and everyone could feel it. Even the students not competing were uneasy, whispering about strategies, points, and who would triumph. Harrison stirred his porridge absently, his mind elsewhere, flicking through wards and countermeasures he had spent the previous nights preparing in the Room of Requirement. The threat of Dumbledoreís plans lingered at the edge of his thoughts. Luna nudged him gently. ØYouíre quiet this morning my Lord,Æ she observed. ØAre you ready?Æ Harrison smiled faintly. ØAs ready as Iíll ever be, little moon. But ready doesnít mean calm.Æ Lunaís lips curved into her signature serene smile. ØCalm isnít always necessary,Æ she said. ØSometimes you just have to be prepared to see the truth unfold.Æ At that moment, the clatter of the staff stepping up to the dais hushed the room. All eyes turned as Professor McGonagall, Headmistress of Hogwarts, raised her hands. The murmurs faded to a respectful silence. ØGood morning,Æ she began, her voice carrying across the Great Hall with the authority and warmth that made the students straighten in their seats. ØToday marks the final stage of the Triwizard Tournament. I know this year has beenè unusual, difficult, and perhaps more dangerous than any of us anticipated. And yet, despite all the trials youíve faced - both in and out of the Tournament - you have proven yourselves capable, courageous, and resilient.Æ She paused, letting the weight of her words settle over the room. ØI am proud of each of you. Your growth, your courage, and your commitment to one another has been extraordinary. Remember, this Tournament may not have unfolded as we expected. Challenges may have arisen that we did not foresee, and dangers may have appeared where we least anticipated them, but you have all rallied together and accomplished so much. So no matter what happens, remember that you are not alone. Hogwarts is your home, and here, you are safe.Æ Harrison felt a small, reassuring warmth spread through him. Despite everything - the chaos, the threats, the undercurrents of danger - Hogwarts did still feel like home to him. He glanced at Luna, who gave him a small, knowing smile. Harrison took a slow sip of his tea, letting the morning settle around him. Today would test them all, not just Fleur and Victor, but every student in the stands. And he would be ready for whatever Dumbledore- and the future- might bring. /// The stands overlooking the maze-like arena were filled with students, staff, and visiting dignitaries, all murmuring with anticipation. Harrison settled into his seat beside Marvolo, his hands resting lightly on the edge of the railing, his senses stretched taut, every nerve alert. He felt butterflies in his stomach, whether from anticipation or nerves he didn't know, what he did know though was that he was ready for this to all be over. Victor Krum was already moving through the first twisting corridor of the task, his movements precise and cautious, his eyes scanning for enchantments and traps. Fleur Delacour had just entered the arena, her elegant strides hiding the careful calculation behind each step. ØSeems like our two remaining champions are eager,Æ Marvolo murmured, leaning close to Harrison. ØThough you seemè distracted. We can scrap the plan if you're not sure.Æ Harrison let his gaze sweep the arena, noting every detail - the shifting walls, the faint shimmer of wards, the subtle enchantments layered over the pathways. ØNo, I'm fine. Iím justè aware,Æ he said. ØI know whatís coming, or at least what to expect. This opportunity is too great of a chance to finally end this..Æ Marvoloís lips pursed, he had hoped that Harrison would back out, though he knew his hope was futile, he could feel the resolve in his Ravens magic, see it in how his eye scanned the crowd - alert, ready, anticipating. ØIf anything goes wrongèÆ Harrison let a small huff escape, though his eyes never left the arena. ØI will be careful. Try to have faith in me.Æ ØItís not a lack of faith, my soul, I simply do not trust that man. He has nothing to lose anymore.Æ ØIf he even so much as touches meè well, he wonít live to regret it. But Iím sure it won't come to that. - I hope it doesn't. I don't want to give him the satisfaction of being proved right.Æ ØProved right?Æ ØThat I am a monster.Æ Marvoloís hand found Harrisonís briefly, giving a light squeeze. ØYou are not a monster. You, my dear soulmate, are beautiful,Æ he said softly, eyes glinting with amusement. ØAnd deadly when you let yourself be. But, there is nothing monstrous about you.Æ Harrisonís gaze flicked to him, a faint heat rising to his cheeks. ØCareful, youíre starting to sound sentimental.Æ ØI am making astute observations, there is nothing sentimental about me,Æ Marvolo replied lightly, though his eyes lingered on Harrison with a sharp intensity thay made him want to blush. A horn sounded from the arena, signalling a particularly dangerous section of the maze. Victor froze mid-step, eyes narrowing as he carefully navigated a series of magically shifting barriers. Fleurís hand flicked through the air, tracing protective charms over herself as she moved towards a blast-end-skewt. Harrison noted the flicker of tension in both their movements, the subtle anxiety of competition, and yet the determination that kept them pressing forward. ØI know what I have to do,Æ Harrison murmured, more to himself than to Marvolo. ØIf he comes for me - No - when he comes, I need to let him think he has a chance. But please don't doubt Iím the one in control. Iíve prepared for this, and I will be the one deciding how it ends.Æ Marvolo chuckled quietly. ØYouíll enjoy this game far more than he ever will.Æ Harrisonís lips twitched into a faint grin. ØIíll enjoy watching him fail. And if he tries anythingè I wonít even break a sweat.Æ The two watched in quiet focus as Fleur manoeuvred around a trapdoor, her hair catching the sunlight streaming into the arena, while Victorís eyes narrowed at a shifting wall of vines laced with subtle hexes. Harrisonís mind was elsewhere, running through contingency after contingency - escape routes, emergency portkeys, enchantments layered over himself and Marvolo, spells ready at the flick of his wrist. He let the sounds of the crowd wash over him, but inside, every fibre of his being was coiled, ready for the moment when the real danger arrived. And he knew it would. /// ØIt's time, Iím going for a walk,Æ he told Marvolo, who rose from his seat beside him, eyes studying him sharply. Marvoloís hand rested briefly on Harrisonís shoulder, a firm, grounding weight. ØHarrison,Æ he said, his voice low but unyielding, Ødonít do anything reckless. You know what Iíve said. If he touches youè if youíre harmed in any way-Æ Harrison glanced at him, lips curling into a small, teasing smirk. ØI know, I know. Youíll burn the world and salt the ashes if anything happens. Relax, Iíll be careful.Æ Marvoloís eyes darkened, the amusement fading. ØI am not joking. You are my world. If you are hurt, I will annihilate everything Dumbledore has ever loved. Understood?Æ Harrison nodded, eyes glinting with a mixture of anticipation and mischief. ØUnderstood. Andè thatís part of why Iím going.Æ Marvolo exhaled slowly, running a hand through his hair. ØYouíre insufferable sometimes, you know that?Æ Harrison smirked, already slipping beyond the castle walls, his mind focused on one thing: drawing the old man out. He walked with deliberate calm, his movements casual, as though he were merely enjoying the afternoon sun, while in reality, every step, every breath, was calculated to make himself an irresistible target. He found a quieter path along the edge of the Black Lake, the trees casting long shadows over the damp earth. Alone, unguardedè the perfect lure. Harrisonís pulse remained steady, his breathing measured. He could feel the faint tug of old, compulsive magic in the air - Dumbledore, somewhere, watching, plotting. And Harrison was ready. A shadow moved behind the trees, subtle at first, then solid. He didnít need to see him to know. ØYou should have stayed with the crowd, you've practically handed yourself over to me you foolish child,Æ a familiar, chilling voice whispered from the darkness. Harrisonís hand hovered near his wand, but he didnít draw it. He tilted his head slightly, eyes cold but steady. ØLetís just get this over with,Æ he muttered, voice calm, almost bored, quiet enough that Albus couldn't quite make out his words. The shadows coalesced, a familiar figure stepping into the pale light. Harrisonís calculated calm held - until in a blink, he was seized, strong hands gripping him from behind. A swirl of magic, a flash of movement, and he was lifted cleanly off the ground. He let himself go, not resisting beyond a light struggle meant to amuse him, not harm. The world tilted as he was whisked away, carried toward the unknown, toward the trap he had long anticipated. And yet, even as the wind whipped past his face, Harrisonís mind was clear. Every contingency, every enchantment he had layered over himself and Marvoloís protections, was running in his head. This was the moment he had waited for. The moment when the old man would overreach. And he was ready. /// Marvolo stood in the stands, fingers tightening around the armrest as he watched the shadows ripple across the path where Harrison had disappeared down. His chest felt tight, a war waging within him - part fear, part anticipation, part something far darker. He had warned him, reminded him, laid out every contingency, every safeguard. Yet here he was, watching Harrison walk into danger willingly. A part of Marvoloís mind screamed at him to intervene, to teleport in and snatch his mate back before a single hair was disturbed. Another part - darker, sharper - relished the thought of what would happen if Dumbledore truly overstepped. His grip tightened, knuckles whitening. The thought of Harrison being harmed - any harm .lit a furnace in his chest. Not just concern, but a raw, elemental fury. Dumbledore had caused enough pain. He had twisted lives, corrupted innocents, and now, if he dared to lay a finger on Harrisonè Everything Dumbledore loved would burn. Every illusion, every possession, every scrap of arrogance would crumble. Marvolo could taste it, the satisfaction, the righteous destruction. He imagined tearing through the world with the old manís lifeblood on his hands, and a part of him felt almostè peaceful. And yet, he restrained himself. He had promised Harrison that the choice was his. The respect, the love, the bond they shared, all demanded he hold back. He had to trust his mate .even if it killed him inside to watch him step into the trap. The wind rustled the trees, carrying a chill across Marvoloís face. He clenched his jaw, holding himself rigid, forcing the dark hunger back down into its cage. The world could wait. Dumbledore would pay, but only if Harrison emerged unscathed, as he always did. Marvolo exhaled slowly, eyes narrowing at the path. His heart hammered in quiet anticipation. The old man was coming. And when he didè Marvoloís lips curved, a smile both dangerous and tender. ØIf he touches you, Harrison,Æ he murmured under his breath, Øhe will learn why the world fears my name.Æ /// The Headmistressís office was filled with an unnatural tension. Minerva, McGonagall, sat rigid in her chair, wand clutched at her side, her mind racing. ØHeís taken him,Æ she whispered, more to herself than anyone else. The words felt impossibly heavy. Across the room, Severusís dark eyes narrowed, fingers drumming on his desk. ØOf course he would,Æ he muttered. ØThe man has no limits, no respect for law or life. Harrison isè a prize to him, not a student.Æ Severus knew the plan, yet a part of him felt unmoored by the uncertainty of it. He hoped nothing would go wrong, not just because Harrison didn't deserve it, but for all the people that loved him. And if a pair of silver eyes and aristocratic features flashed in his mind - well that was nobody's business but his own. Pomfrey, standing near the doorway, paled at Minerva's announcement. ØButè the wards .surely they wouldnít -Æ Her voice faltered, the rest of the sentence lost as panic prickled at her spine. McGonagallís gaze hardened. ØHe must have used magic beyond anything we originally anticipated. Thereís no other explanation. He bypassed the protections, the charm work, the tracking spells. He knows the Castle too well.Æ Flitwickís hands were trembling slightly, a rare show of anger. ØButè but heís only a boy. And Dumbledore - he - he wouldnítèÆ His words trailed off, though the look on his face promised violence if he found Dumbledore. ØA boy yes, but the most important boy in the world right now,Æ Minerva said grimly. ØIf he harms Harrisonè if anything happensèÆ Her jaw clenched. The unspoken thought hovered in the room: Dumbledore cannot be allowed to succeed. Snapeís eyes flicked to McGonagall. ØWe need a plan. Immediately. What to tell people, the students, the press, we have the eyes of the world on us right now..Æ The staff exchanged glances - shock, fear, determination. The whispers that had already begun among the students in the Great Hall, now found their echo among the teachers. But one thing was certain, Dumbledore had crossed a line, and the consequences were about to ripple through every corner of the wizarding world. McGonagall stood abruptly, wand in hand, voice sharp and commanding. ØPrepare the wards. Notify anyone who can help. And pray to lady magic - hope that every enchantment, every bit of knowledge we possess, might be enough to help bring him back.Æ The room fell into tense, frenzied motion. Teachers who had once been calm, authoritative figures were now united by a single, desperate goal -save Harrison before it was too late. /// The quiet of the unused classroom was a sharp contrast to the chaos rippling through the castle. Sirius paced the floor, hands clenching and unclenching, while Severus leaned against the wall, arms crossed, his expression unreadable - but tense. ØIè I donít know if I can do this,Æ Sirius admitted finally, voice low and tight, the vulnerability in it unusual for the notorious Black. ØEven if this is the planè even if Harrison can handle himselfè I canít lose him. Not like this. Not to Dumbledore.Æ Severusís eyes softened imperceptibly, and he took a measured step forward. ØHarrison is strong,Æ he said quietly, deliberately, each word carrying weight. ØStronger than we can imagine. He will survive this - he always does.Æ Sirius ran a hand through his hair, frustrated and fearful. ØI shouldnít be scared. I shouldnítè but I am. Heís my son, Severus. My boy.Æ Severusís gaze lingered, steady and firm, and for the first time that Sirius could remember, he allowed himself to look unguarded. ØAnd that is why he will be okay, because he has you,Æ Severus said softly. ØBecause he knows you're waiting for him to return. Because he knows he is loved.Æ The air shifted, charged and intimate, and Sirius found himself stepping closer, drawn by something he hadnít let himself truly acknowledge, by the intensity in Severusís quiet confidence, by the shared fear for the boy they both loved. And then, before either could fully process it, Sirius closed the distance, brushing his lips against Severusís in a quick, searching kiss. Shock flickered across both of their faces, a mix of surprise and something unspoken - relief, tension, desire. Severus froze for a heartbeat, then allowed the moment to linger, responding just enough before pulling back, his eyes dark and unreadable, yet betraying the smallest flicker of vulnerability. ØIè didnít expect that,Æ Sirius admitted, voice husky. ØNo,Æ Severus said quietly, the corner of his mouth twitching in the faintest semblance of a smile. ØNeither did I.Æ But beneath the shock and hesitation, both of them knew something had shifted, an unspoken understanding that even amidst the chaos and the threat to Harrison, they werenít entirely alone. /// Amelia sat at her desk, hands gripping the edge so tightly her knuckles were white, as the message came through. Harrison had been taken. Kidnapped. She exhaled sharply, leaning back in her chair, her mind racing and yet, somehow, narrowing to that one inescapable truth -of course itís him. ØEverythingè everything always comes back to him,Æ she muttered under her breath, her voice tight with frustration. ØEvery plan, every trap, every schemeè it always ends up with him in the middle. Heís likeè he has the worst luck alive, or maybe heís just irresistible to chaos.Æ The thought made her chest ache. She had spent years trying to protect others, trying to untangle Dumbledoreís web, and yet time and again, Harrison seemed to stumble straight into the storm. Her fingers drummed against the desk, impatient and angry. ØItís never just about the world, is it?Æ she whispered to herself. ØItís always about him. AlwaysèÆ Even as she scolded herself for the sentiment, part of her felt a reluctant awe - he was always at the centre, and somehow, and against all odds, he survived. Somehow, she had no doubt he would survive this too. But the weight of it -the worry, the helplessness- pressed down on her like a physical thing. Amelia closed her eyes for a moment, breathing through it, steeling herself. The world might seemingly revolve around Harrison, and his luck might always draw disaster, but she still had work to do. She would find him. And she would make sure that this time, nothing could touch him. /// Harrison blinked awake, the harsh light of the chamber burning his eyes. He was alone - or almost alone. At the far end of the room, Dumbledore paced, hands wringing and voice sharp, each word slicing the air. Øèand you -you were supposed to be the hero! My plan! Everything was meant to lead to you taking your rightful place, and insteadèÆ Dumbledoreís voice cracked, then rose again, ØYouíve ruined it all! All of it! Every carefully laid scheme has been destroyed because you are stubbornè obstinateè weak!Æ Harrisonís eyes narrowed, assessing the older man with a cool, unshakeable calm. The frantic energy radiating off Dumbledore only sharpened his clarity. He had expected theatrics - he had expected threats - but not this raw, almost childlike rage. ØYou donít get to call me weak,Æ Harrison said softly, though Dumbledore didnít seem hear him over his own tirade. ØYou! Youíre to blame! Everything is your fault!Æ Dumbledore shouted, pointing a trembling finger. ØTom's return, the chaos, the delay in bringing balance to the world - itís because you refused to follow my designs! And now -now you sit there, comfortable, taunting fate instead of embracing it! You, you're just Tomís whore! You would be better dead than seduced by the Dark Sideè if only you had allowed yourself to embrace your destiny, to be controlled!Æ Harrison let the words wash over him, silently cataloguing the venom, the delusion. For a long moment he said nothing, letting Dumbledore exhaust himself, letting the old man twist and turn in his own anger. Finally, Harrison rose, smooth and deliberate, the sound of his boots echoing on the stone floor. ØEnough.Æ Dumbledore stopped mid-gesture, eyes wide, chest heaving. ØWhat-?Æ Harrison moved forward, each step measured, and with a flick of his fingers the bindings still restraining him fell away. He raised his hand, fingers curling, and a curse shot forward, searing through the air and landing squarely on Dumbledore. The older man hissed and recoiled, his own magic twisting violently before dissipating harmlessly against Harrisonís flared aura. The punishment from lady magic was still going strong. ØIn another life,Æ Harrison said, voice low and deliberate, Øyour plans might have worked. You might have bent the world to your design. But this isnít that life.Æ He glanced down at his wrist, a glinting bracelet pulsing softly with enchantments. A touch, a word whispered, and a shimmer of light exploded across the room. The signal would reach Marvolo. Dumbledore staggered back, eyes wild. ØYou- what have you done? You will regret this! You think yourself smart, but the light-Æ Harrison didnít let him finish. Another spell was sent, Incarcerous. Calm, precise, and cold, he turned away from the man who had tried to control his life. The bracelet hummed at his command, and the world outside would soon answer. Marvolo would come. And they would end this. One way or another. /// The air shimmered, and a sudden ripple of power announced Marvoloís arrival before he even stepped fully into the chamber. Harrisonís bracelet glowed briefly, marking the culmination of his call. Marvoloís eyes immediately took in the scene - Dumbledore on his knees, snarling and sputtering, his magic flaring weakly against Harrisonís Incarcerous, while Harrison stood calm and unshaken. ØHarrison,Æ Marvolo said softly, stepping forward and closing the distance between them in just a few strides. His voice, low and intimate, carried only to Harrisonís ears. ØAre youè unharmed, my dear?Æ Harrison exhaled, shoulders truly relaxing for the first time since waking up to the old man and his tirade. ØI am,Æ he said, his voice steady. ØI told you this would all work outÆ Marvoloís gaze softened as he scanned Harrison for any sign of injury or fatigue, fingertips brushing against Harrisonís face in a reassuring touch. ØGood. Thatís all that matters.Æ From the floor, Dumbledoreís voice shrieked, furious. ØItís all a trick! Itís all fake! The Dark Lord canít love! He canít understand it - he canít!Æ Marvoloís lips curved into the faintest of smiles, and his eyes flicked toward Harrison. ØThat may have been trueè once. But heís wrong now. Youè youíve fixed me. Youíve healed my soul, Harrison.Æ Dumbledore froze, eyes widening in horror as understanding dawned. ØNoè noè you .youíve absorbed the Horcruxes! You can't have, you wouldnít have! I don't believe you!Æ Marvoloís hand moved to Harrisonís shoulder, firm and grounding. ØBut I have. And now, you-Æ he gestured to Dumbledore, Øare powerless.Æ Desperation twisted Dumbledoreís face as he realised the gravity of the situation, he turned his plea to Harrison. ØYou need to kill him! Strike him down! Fight for the light! You - you owe it to the world! He killed your parents!Æ Harrison laughed, low and certain, the sound echoing through the chamber. ØMaybe, but I would never harm my soulmate.Æ The old man seemed to draw power in his desperation and a screamed curse rattled through the stone walls, but Harrison and Marvolo moved in tandem, their magic weaving protective barriers around them both. Together, they cast a series of counter-curses at Dumbledore, forcing him back to his knees, exhausted and cowering. Harrison tilted his head, a decisive glint in his eyes. ØYouíve done enough damage, but your end doesnít have to be violent. I think it would be poetic if the DMLE took you. We're going to let them arrest you. Let the world see what you truly are.Æ Dumbledoreís face twisted in rage, his words choked with disbelief. ØNo, it can't end like this. You have to believe me, my boy. He needs to die!Æ Harrison tutted, Øit's such a shame when the mighty fall.Æ Marvolo smirked, glancing at Harrison, his expression soft but fierce. ØIíll see you soon my love,Æ he promised. Harrison nodded, before activating their final charm, and this time it was Dumbledore who took an unwilling portkey. /// The Great Hall was in near-panic. Students whispered nervously among themselves, some pacing, some craning their necks toward the windows. Teachers scanned the grounds, aurors were stationed at every entrance, and every eye seemed fixed on the shadows. Harrison had been missing for hours - kidnapped, it was assumed. Then, with a sudden pop of displaced air and the faint smell of ozone, a portkey landed near the front doors. Harrison stumbled out, dragging an unconscious Dumbledore behind him. His robes were rumpled, his hand brushing his hair back from his face as though trying to compose himself. He moved with precise control, but his posture and the slight tremor in his steps made it believable that he had endured something harrowing. ØHarrison!Æ several students cried, rushing forward, but he raised a hand, signalling them to stop. His eyes darted around the hall, taking in the anxious faces, the worried gazes of his friends, and the stern, watchful presence of Amelia and Minerva at the staff table. He took a few deliberate steps to the centre, allowing Dumbledore's prone body to hit the floor without care. ØIè Iím fine,Æ he said, letting out a breath he hadnít realised heíd been holding. ØDumbledore tried to take meè he did take me - but heís no longer a threat. Heís unconscious nowè.Æ The room erupted in murmurs. Some whispered his name in awe, others exhaled in relief, and a few stared in disbelief at the sight of Dumbledore lying on the floor, bound by the magical restraints that Harrison had left in place. Amelia moved forward swiftly, her cloak brushing the floor. ØHarrison,Æ she said, her voice sharp but tinged with concern. ØAre you hurt? Andè What about him?Æ She gestured toward the motionless figure of Dumbledore. ØIím fine,Æ Harrison assured her, his hands trembling just enough to sell the story. ØHeís alive. I cursed him a bitè I didn't want to, but he wouldn't stop and I just wanted to get away.Æ All around him, students and staff alike exchanged looks of astonishment, so tinged with pride and others worried. The younger ones clutched each other silently, realising the gravity of what had just occurred. Teachers nodded at each other, relief mixed with admiration. Minerva, standing tall at the staff table, allowed a faint smile to cross her usually stern features. ØIím glad you're okay Harrison,Æ she said, her tone carrying the weight of a thousand unspoken worries. ØDonít worry about him - you did what you had to do, the threat has been neutralised. That is what matters.Æ Harrison gave a small nod, his gaze sweeping the hall as if drinking in the moment. The portkey still glimmered faintly behind him, the telltale sign of the journey he had endured. Every pair of eyes was on him, knowing he had been kidnapped, knowing he had faced Dumbledore - and returned with him subdued. He paused, letting his chest rise and fall slowly, grounding himself in the reality of the moment. Then, with a final glance at the anxious faces around him, he carefully stumbled, as if the weight of it all had finally hit him. His performance seemed to jolt Amelia, and soon the aurors approached to take custody of the fallen former headmaster. /// The doors of the Great Hall swung open with purpose, Amelia had summoned a full DMLE response team, their presence snapping the murmurs into tense silence. Wards shimmered faintly as they spread out, trained professionals already assessing the scene. Ameliaís gaze was steely, locked on the unconscious figure on the floor. With little fanfare she Rennervated him. ØAlbus Dumbledore,Æ she said flatly. ØYouíre under arrest.Æ The aurors moved as one. Magical restraints snapped into place - ancient, heavy things meant for legends rather than men. Dumbledore stirred weakly as they lifted him, a low sound escaping his throat, but he did not seem to wake fully. The sight of him bound, powerless, sent another ripple of shock through the watching students. Harrison took a small step back as they worked, shoulders tight, breathing shallow. He let his hands shake when Amelia turned back to him - let his eyes fill with tears. ØHarrison,Æ she said gently now, voice deliberately softened. ØYou did well. But youíre done for the moment. Youíre going to the hospital wing.Æ ØI - yes,Æ he replied, swallowing hard, eyes flicking briefly to the door as if still expecting Dumbledore to rise. ØI thinkè I think that would be good.Æ Minerva was at his side instantly, her hand firm at his elbow. ØYouíre safe now, Mr. Potter-Black,Æ she said, loud enough for the room to hear. ØNo one will touch you again.Æ The students watched as he was guided away- some with awe, some with fear, some with unmistakable relief. Whispers followed him like a tide. ØHe fought DumbledoreèÆ ØHe survivedèÆ ØHe brought him backèÆ Harrison kept his head bowed, posture closed, playing the part of the shaken survivor perfectly. The moment the hospital wing doors closed behind him, the noise of the castle muffled into distant quiet. Poppy Pomfrey descended immediately, wand already out. ØSit,Æ she ordered, brisk and no-nonsense. ØNow.Æ Harrison obeyed, settling onto the bed. The instant Minervaís back was turned and Poppyís spells focused elsewhere, the tension drained from him in one smooth breath. His shoulders loosened. His pulse slowed. Inside, there was only calm. Itís over, he thought. Heís finished. Poppy frowned faintly as diagnostic spells washed over him. ØExhaustion, mild magical strain, elevated stress responseè nothing serious.Æ She eyed him sharply. ØYouíre lucky.Æ Harrison offered a small, tired smile - the safe kind. ØI know.Æ Minerva lingered near the doorway, her reflection visible in the glass cabinet. Relief warred with fury on her face, but she said nothing, only nodded once before leaving them to it. This should never of happened. When the room settled and the last of the spells faded, Harrison leaned back against the pillows, eyes closing briefly. Behind the mask of tremors and shock, there was only controlled satisfaction. Dumbledore was alive. Dumbledore was arrested. And Harrison had won. /// The hospital wing settled into silence. Curtains rustled softly as Madam Pomfrey made her rounds elsewhere, footsteps fading until Harrison was alone at last. The moment the door clicked shut, the faade fell away. He slumped back against the pillows, not dramatically - justè honestly. The careful tremors stopped. The shallow breathing evened out. The wide, startled eyes softened into something sharp and thoughtful. For a long moment, he simply stared at the ceiling. Itís done. The words didnít come with triumph. They came with weight. He lifted his hands, studying them as if they belonged to someone else. These were the same hands that had trembled convincingly in the Great Hall, that had dragged Dumbledoreís unconscious body through a portkey, that had called Marvolo without hesitation. Steady hands. Decisive hands. Hands that had chosen. A quiet laugh escaped him - low, almost disbelieving. ØMerlin,Æ he murmured. ØI really did it.Æ The anger came next, sharp and sudden. Not explosive - controlled. Refined. He thought of Dumbledoreís ranting, the way the man had looked at him not as a person but as a failed weapon. A hero that hadnít followed the script. In another life, you would have won, Harrison thought calmly. You almost did. That truth didnít scare him anymore. What scared him - what had always scared him - was how easy it would have been to kill him. How natural it had felt, standing there with that much power humming under his skin. How a darker part of him had leaned forward, eager, curious. He pressed his palm flat against his chest, feeling the steady beat of his heart. I didnít do it, he reminded himself. I chose not to. Not because Dumbledore deserved mercy. Not because killing was wrong. But because Harrison knew himself now. If he had crossed that final line, he wouldnít have stopped. Not with Dumbledore. Not ever. And he had fought too hard - lived too many lives - to become someone ruled by that hunger. A faint warmth curled through him then, grounding and familiar. He knows Iím safe. Marvolo. The bond hummed quietly, satisfied but alert. Harrison allowed himself a small, private smile. ØYou worried anyway,Æ he murmured, fond and certain. He closed his eyes, exhaustion finally catching up with him - not the bone-deep kind, but the kind that comes after holding the world together with sheer will and letting go at last. When he opened them again, his gaze was clear. Dumbledore was finished. The past was finished. And Harrison had stepped fully into who he was meant to be. Not a hero. Not a weapon. A lord in his own right. And for the first time in a very long while, he rested - not because the fight was over, but because he trusted himself to face whatever came next. /// The hospital wing was unusually quiet, sunlight spilling through the tall windows and settling in warm patches across the white sheets. Harrison sat propped against his pillows, a cup of tea cooling untouched at his side, when the curtain rustled softly. Neville appeared first, shoulders tense until he saw Harrison upright and breathing. ØYouíre-Æ he stopped himself, swallowing hard. ØYouíre really okay?Æ ØI am,Æ Harrison said gently. ØI promise.Æ Draco followed, arms crossed tightly as he leaned against the foot of the bed, pale but composed. His eyes swept over Harrison in a quick, assessing glance - wards intact, magic steady. Only then did some of the tightness leave his posture. ØYouíre an idiot,Æ he said flatly. ØBut a very effective one.Æ Susan and Hannah hovered just behind him, relief plain on their faces, while Blaise gave Harrison a slow, approving nod. The twins lingered near the door, uncharacteristically quiet, grins subdued by the weight of what might have gone wrong. Luna slipped in last, serene as ever, pale hair catching the light. She moved to Harrisonís side and tilted her head, studying him with unblinking focus. ØYouíre not broken,Æ she said calmly. ØJust a littleè stretched. Like magic taffy.Æ Harrison snorted despite himself. ØIíll take that.Æ Luna smiled faintly, then added, ØBumblebees only get one sting. After that, they fall apart. Itís sad, but alsoè inevitable.Æ The room went still. Neville let out a slow breath. ØSoè itís really over?Æ Harrison met their eyes one by one. ØYes. He wonít get another chance.Æ They all knew - every single one of them - that this hadnít been luck. That Harrison had walked into the danger with open eyes and layered contingencies. None of them said it aloud, but the knowledge sat heavy and shared between them. Harrison was lucky that everything had gone to plan. Draco exhaled sharply, running a hand through his hair. ØI know you planned this, but I was terrified,Æ he said quietly. Harrison felt a measure of guilt, ØI know, and I'm sorry, but if it helps I also planned to survive.Æ ØIt doesn't, but that's okay, it just shows how much you mean to usÆ Susan said softly, voice thick with relief. Neville stepped closer, hands clenched at his sides. ØYou scared us.Æ ØI know,Æ Harrison said, and meant the apology. Luna reached out and briefly squeezed his fingers. ØPlans that end storms always look frightening from inside the cloud,Æ she said dreamily. ØBut you landed on your feet.Æ The tension finally broke. The room filled with quiet laughter, soft voices, the kind of warmth that only comes after surviving something terrible together. Harrison leaned back against the pillows, surrounded by them, feeling the weight lift just a little more. Chapter End Notes Did I use too many chess metaphors? probably.
